=== L07_InjectiveNonMembership.lean ===
     1	import Game.Metadata
     2	
     3	World "PsetFinset"
     4	Level 7
     5	
     6	Title "Injective Non-Membership"
     7	
     8	Introduction "
     9	# Injectivity Preserves Non-Membership
    10	
    11	If $f$ is injective and $a \\notin s$, then $f(a) \\notin f(s)$.
    12	
    13	Intuitively: if $a$ isn't in the original set, its image can't
    14	appear in the image set (because no other element maps to $f(a)$
    15	when $f$ is injective).
    16	
    17	The proof uses backward image extraction and injectivity to derive
    18	a contradiction.
    19	"
    20	
    21	/-- Injective functions preserve non-membership. -/
    22	Statement (f : ℕ → ℕ) (hf : Function.Injective f) (s : Finset ℕ)
    23	    (a : ℕ) (hna : a ∉ s) : f a ∉ s.image f := by
    24	  Hint "The goal is `f a ∉ s.image f`, which is a negation.
    25	  Use `intro hfa` to assume `f a ∈ s.image f` and derive `False`."
    26	  intro hfa
    27	  Hint (hidden := true) "Extract the witness: `rw [Finset.mem_image] at hfa`
    28	  then `obtain ⟨b, hbs, hfb⟩ := hfa`. Apply injectivity:
    29	  `have hab : b = a := hf hfb`. Rewrite and contradict:
    30	  `rw [hab] at hbs; exact hna hbs`."
    31	  rw [Finset.mem_image] at hfa
    32	  obtain ⟨b, hbs, hfb⟩ := hfa
    33	  have hab : b = a := hf hfb
    34	  rw [hab] at hbs
    35	  exact hna hbs
    36	
    37	Conclusion "
    38	The proof pattern:
    39	1. Assume $f(a) \\in f(s)$ — extract a witness $b \\in s$ with $f(b) = f(a)$
    40	2. Apply injectivity: $f(b) = f(a) \\implies b = a$
    41	3. Rewrite: $b \\in s$ becomes $a \\in s$, contradicting $a \\notin s$
    42	
    43	This is a different use of injectivity from cardinality preservation —
    44	here injectivity creates a contradiction rather than establishing
    45	a count.
    46	"
    47	
    48	/-- `Finset.not_mem_image_of_injective` states that if `f` is injective
    49	and `a ∉ s`, then `f a ∉ s.image f`. -/
    50	TheoremDoc Finset.not_mem_image_of_injective as "Finset.not_mem_image_of_injective" in "Finset"

=== L08_FilterImageContainment.lean ===
     1	import Game.Metadata
     2	
     3	World "PsetFinset"
     4	Level 8
     5	
     6	Title "Filter Image Containment"
     7	
     8	Introduction "
     9	# Filter + Image Integration
    10	
    11	Prove that filtering a set and then taking its image gives a
    12	subset of the image of the original set:
    13	
    14	$$(s.\\text{filter}\\; p).\\text{image}\\; f \\;\\subseteq\\; s.\\text{image}\\; f$$
    15	
    16	The proof combines backward image extraction, filter unwrapping,
    17	and forward image construction.
    18	"
    19	
    20	/-- Filtering before imaging gives a subset of imaging directly. -/
    21	Statement (s : Finset ℕ) (p : ℕ → Prop) [DecidablePred p] (f : ℕ → ℕ) :
    22	    (s.filter p).image f ⊆ s.image f := by
    23	  rw [Finset.subset_iff]
    24	  intro y hy
    25	  Hint "Extract the witness from `hy` using backward image membership."
    26	  Hint (hidden := true) "`rw [Finset.mem_image] at hy` then
    27	  `obtain ⟨x, hx, hfx⟩ := hy`. Unfold the filter:
    28	  `rw [Finset.mem_filter] at hx`. Build the goal:
    29	  `rw [Finset.mem_image]; exact ⟨x, hx.1, hfx⟩`."
    30	  rw [Finset.mem_image] at hy
    31	  obtain ⟨x, hx, hfx⟩ := hy
    32	  rw [Finset.mem_filter] at hx
    33	  rw [Finset.mem_image]
    34	  exact ⟨x, hx.1, hfx⟩
    35	
    36	Conclusion "
    37	The key insight: the witness `x` works for both sides. From the
    38	filtered image, `x ∈ s.filter p` means `x ∈ s ∧ p x`. The filter
    39	predicate `p x` is discarded — only `x ∈ s` is needed for the
    40	unfiltered image.
    41	
    42	This mirrors L03 (filter preserves containment) but with image
    43	instead of intersection: filtering can only shrink, so the image
    44	of the filtered set is contained in the image of the whole set.
    45	"
    46	
    47	/-- `Finset.filter_subset p s` states that `s.filter p ⊆ s`:
    48	filtering can only remove elements, never add them. -/
    49	TheoremDoc Finset.filter_subset as "Finset.filter_subset" in "Finset"

=== L09_ImageUnionSdiff.lean ===
     1	import Game.Metadata
     2	
     3	World "PsetFinset"
     4	Level 9
     5	
     6	Title "Image, Union, and Set Difference"
     7	
     8	Introduction "
     9	# Union + Image + Sdiff Integration
    10	
    11	Prove that removing the image of $s$ from the image of $s \\cup t$
    12	leaves only elements from the image of $t$:
    13	
    14	$$f(s \\cup t) \\setminus f(s) \\;\\subseteq\\; f(t)$$
    15	
    16	This integrates moves from FinsetOperations and FinsetImage:
    17	subset, sdiff, union, image backward/forward, cases, and by_contra.
    18	
    19	The interesting case: when the witness came from $s$, you can
    20	build $y \\in f(s)$, which contradicts $y \\notin f(s)$.
    21	"
    22	
    23	/-- Elements in the image of a union but not in the image of s must come from t. -/
    24	Statement (f : ℕ → ℕ) (s t : Finset ℕ) :
    25	    (s ∪ t).image f \ s.image f ⊆ t.image f := by
    26	  rw [Finset.subset_iff]
    27	  intro y hy
    28	  rw [Finset.mem_sdiff] at hy
    29	  obtain ⟨hut, hns⟩ := hy
    30	  Hint "Extract the witness from `hut` using backward image membership,
    31	  then determine whether the witness came from `s` or `t`."
    32	  rw [Finset.mem_image] at hut
    33	  obtain ⟨x, hx, hfx⟩ := hut
    34	  rw [Finset.mem_union] at hx
    35	  Hint (hidden := true) "Case-split on `hx` with `cases hx with`.
    36	  In the `inl` case (`x ∈ s`): use `by_contra` then `apply hns`
    37	  to show `y ∈ s.image f`, contradicting `hns`.
    38	  In the `inr` case (`x ∈ t`): build forward membership directly."
    39	  cases hx with
    40	  | inl hxs =>
    41	    by_contra _
    42	    apply hns
    43	    rw [Finset.mem_image]
    44	    exact ⟨x, hxs, hfx⟩
    45	  | inr hxt =>
    46	    rw [Finset.mem_image]
    47	    exact ⟨x, hxt, hfx⟩
    48	
    49	Conclusion "
    50	This proof integrated 6 moves from two worlds:
    51	
    52	| Move | Source |
    53	|---|---|
    54	| `rw [Finset.subset_iff]; intro y hy` | FinsetOperations |
    55	| `rw [Finset.mem_sdiff] at hy` | FinsetOperations |
    56	| `rw [Finset.mem_image] at hut; obtain ...` | FinsetImage |
    57	| `rw [Finset.mem_union] at hx; cases ...` | FinsetOperations |
    58	| `by_contra; apply hns` | FinsetOperations |
    59	| `rw [Finset.mem_image]; exact ⟨x, ...⟩` | FinsetImage |
    60	
    61	The `inl` case used `by_contra` to expose the contradiction:
    62	if $x \\in s$, then $y = f(x) \\in f(s)$, contradicting $y \\notin f(s)$.
    63	"
    64	
    65	/-- `sdiff_le_self` states `a \ b ≤ a` in a lattice. -/
    66	TheoremDoc sdiff_le_self as "sdiff_le_self" in "Order"
    67	
    68	/-- `Finset.sdiff_subset` states `s \ t ⊆ s`. -/
    69	TheoremDoc Finset.sdiff_subset as "Finset.sdiff_subset" in "Finset"
    70	
    71	/-- `sdiff_le` states `a \ b ≤ a` (alternate name for sdiff_le_self). -/
    72	TheoremDoc sdiff_le as "sdiff_le" in "Order"

=== L10_Boss.lean ===
     1	import Game.Metadata
     2	
     3	World "PsetFinset"
     4	Level 10
     5	
     6	Title "Boss: Image Preserves Set Difference"
     7	
     8	Introduction "
     9	# Boss: Image and Set Difference Under Injectivity
    10	
    11	Given an **injective** function $f$, prove:
    12	
    13	$$(s \\setminus t).\\text{image}\\; f \\;\\subseteq\\; s.\\text{image}\\; f \\;\\setminus\\; t.\\text{image}\\; f$$
    14	
    15	If you remove elements of $t$ from $s$ *before* applying $f$,
    16	the result is contained in what you'd get by applying $f$ to $s$
    17	and then removing the image of $t$.
    18	
    19	This proof integrates 7+ moves from FinsetOperations and FinsetImage.
    20	No new moves — only moves you've already used.
    21	"
    22	
    23	/-- Under injectivity, image preserves set difference (as a subset). -/
    24	Statement (f : ℕ → ℕ) (hf : Function.Injective f) (s t : Finset ℕ) :
    25	    (s \ t).image f ⊆ s.image f \ t.image f := by
    26	  rw [Finset.subset_iff]
    27	  intro y hy
    28	  Hint "Extract the witness from `hy`, unfold the source sdiff,
    29	  then build both parts of the target sdiff."
    30	  rw [Finset.mem_image] at hy
    31	  obtain ⟨a, ha, hfa⟩ := hy
    32	  rw [Finset.mem_sdiff] at ha
    33	  rw [Finset.mem_sdiff]
    34	  constructor
    35	  -- Part 1: y ∈ s.image f
    36	  · Hint (hidden := true) "`rw [Finset.mem_image]; exact ⟨a, ha.1, hfa⟩`"
    37	    rw [Finset.mem_image]
    38	    exact ⟨a, ha.1, hfa⟩
    39	  -- Part 2: y ∉ t.image f
    40	  · Hint "The goal is `y ∉ t.image f`. Assume `y ∈ t.image f`,
    41	    extract a witness `b ∈ t`, and use injectivity to derive `a = b`."
    42	    Hint (hidden := true) "`intro hyt; rw [Finset.mem_image] at hyt;
    43	    obtain ⟨b, hbt, hfb⟩ := hyt`. Then
    44	    `have hfab : f a = f b := by rw [hfa, hfb]`.
    45	    `have hab : a = b := hf hfab`.
    46	    `rw [hab] at ha; exact ha.2 hbt`."
    47	    intro hyt
    48	    rw [Finset.mem_image] at hyt
    49	    obtain ⟨b, hbt, hfb⟩ := hyt
    50	    have hfab : f a = f b := by rw [hfa, hfb]
    51	    have hab : a = b := hf hfab
    52	    rw [hab] at ha
    53	    exact ha.2 hbt
    54	
    55	Conclusion "
    56	Congratulations — you've completed the **Finset Problem Set**!
    57	
    58	You proved that injective functions preserve set difference:
    59	$f(s \\setminus t) \\subseteq f(s) \\setminus f(t)$ when $f$ is injective.
    60	
    61	The key insight: injectivity was essential. Without it, two
    62	different elements $a \\notin t$ and $b \\in t$ could map to
    63	the same $y$, making $y$ appear in $t.\\text{image}\\; f$ even though
    64	it came from an element outside $t$. Injectivity forces $a = b$,
    65	which creates the contradiction.
    66	
    67	**What's next**: The next world introduces **cardinality** —
    68	the number of elements in a finset — and the fundamental
    69	counting identities.
    70	"
    71	
    72	/-- `Finset.image_sdiff s t hf` states that when `f` is injective,
    73	`(s \\ t).image f = s.image f \\ t.image f` — image distributes
    74	over set difference under injectivity. -/
    75	TheoremDoc Finset.image_sdiff as "Finset.image_sdiff" in "Finset"
    76	
    77	/-- `Finset.image_sdiff_of_injOn` is the `InjOn` variant of
    78	`Finset.image_sdiff` — requires injectivity only on the relevant sets. -/
    79	TheoremDoc Finset.image_sdiff_of_injOn as "Finset.image_sdiff_of_injOn" in "Finset"
    80

=== L02_DoubleComplementSubset.lean ===
     1	import Game.Metadata
     2	
     3	World "PsetFinset"
     4	Level 2
     5	
     6	Title "Double Complement Under Subset"
     7	
     8	Introduction "
     9	# Set Difference with a Subset Constraint
    10	
    11	When $t \\subseteq s$, the double complement simplifies:
    12	
    13	$$s \\setminus (s \\setminus t) = t$$
    14	
    15	Removing from $s$ everything not in $t$, then taking that away
    16	from $s$, recovers exactly $t$ (not just $s \\cap t$, because
    17	every element of $t$ is already in $s$).
    18	
    19	This is an `ext` proof. The **backward direction** will require
    20	`by_contra` — you'll need to derive `x \\in t` from a negated
    21	hypothesis.
    22	"
    23	
    24	/-- Under subset, double complement recovers the subset exactly. -/
    25	Statement (s t : Finset ℕ) (hts : t ⊆ s) : s \ (s \ t) = t := by
    26	  Hint "Start with `ext x` then `constructor`."
    27	  ext x
    28	  constructor
    29	  -- Forward: x ∈ s \ (s \ t) → x ∈ t
    30	  · Hint "Use `intro h` and unfold with `rw [Finset.mem_sdiff] at h`."
    31	    intro h
    32	    rw [Finset.mem_sdiff] at h
    33	    obtain ⟨hs, hst⟩ := h
    34	    Hint "You have `hs : x ∈ s` and `hst : x ∉ s \\\\ t`. The goal
    35	    is `x ∈ t` — a positive statement you can't extract directly."
    36	    Hint (hidden := true) "Use `by_contra hnt` to assume `x ∉ t`.
    37	    Then build `x ∈ s \\\\ t` from `hs` and `hnt` to contradict `hst`:
    38	    `apply hst; rw [Finset.mem_sdiff]; exact ⟨hs, hnt⟩`."
    39	    by_contra hnt
    40	    apply hst
    41	    rw [Finset.mem_sdiff]
    42	    exact ⟨hs, hnt⟩
    43	  -- Backward: x ∈ t → x ∈ s \ (s \ t)
    44	  · Hint (hidden := true) "Use `intro hxt`, then `rw [Finset.mem_sdiff]`
    45	    and `constructor`. First part: use `hts` to get `x ∈ s`.
    46	    Second part: `intro h; rw [Finset.mem_sdiff] at h; exact h.2 hxt`."
    47	    intro hxt
    48	    rw [Finset.mem_sdiff]
    49	    constructor
    50	    · rw [Finset.subset_iff] at hts
    51	      exact hts hxt
    52	    · intro h
    53	      rw [Finset.mem_sdiff] at h
    54	      exact h.2 hxt
    55	
    56	Conclusion "
    57	You proved $t \\subseteq s \\implies s \\setminus (s \\setminus t) = t$.
    58	
    59	The forward direction required `by_contra`: the goal `x \\in t` is
    60	positive, but your hypotheses only give `x \\in s` and
    61	`x \\notin s \\setminus t`. Assuming `x \\notin t` lets you build
    62	`x \\in s \\setminus t`, contradicting the second hypothesis.
    63	
    64	The backward direction used the subset hypothesis to get `x \\in s`,
    65	then showed `x \\in s \\setminus t` is impossible because it would
    66	give `x \\notin t`.
    67	"
    68

     1	import Game.Metadata
     2	
     3	World "PsetFinset"
     4	Level 3
     5	
     6	Title "Filter Preserves Containment"
     7	
     8	Introduction "
     9	# Filter + Intersection Integration
    10	
    11	If you start with a smaller set ($s \\cap t$ instead of $s$), then
    12	filtering gives a smaller result:
    13	
    14	$$(s \\cap t).\\text{filter}\\; p \\;\\subseteq\\; s.\\text{filter}\\; p$$
    15	
    16	The proof is element-chasing: unfold filter and intersection,
    17	extract the parts, reassemble what you need.
    18	"
    19	
    20	/-- Filtering an intersection gives a subset of filtering the first set. -/
    21	Statement (s t : Finset ℕ) (p : ℕ → Prop) [DecidablePred p] :
    22	    (s ∩ t).filter p ⊆ s.filter p := by
    23	  Hint "Subset goal: `rw [Finset.subset_iff]` then `intro x hx`."
    24	  rw [Finset.subset_iff]
    25	  intro x hx
    26	  Hint (hidden := true) "Unfold `hx` with `rw [Finset.mem_filter] at hx`,
    27	  then `obtain ⟨hst, hp⟩ := hx`.
    28	  Unfold `hst` with `rw [Finset.mem_inter] at hst`.
    29	  Build the goal: `rw [Finset.mem_filter]; exact ⟨hst.1, hp⟩`."
    30	  rw [Finset.mem_filter] at hx
    31	  obtain ⟨hst, hp⟩ := hx
    32	  rw [Finset.mem_inter] at hst
    33	  rw [Finset.mem_filter]
    34	  constructor
    35	  · exact hst.1
    36	  · exact hp
    37	
    38	Conclusion "
    39	The pattern: unfold everything, extract the parts, reassemble.
    40	
    41	The `x ∈ t` component was extracted but not needed — it came along
    42	for the ride from the intersection but isn't required for the right-hand
    43	filter. Keeping exactly the parts you need is the essence of subset proofs.
    44	"
    45	
    46	/-- `Finset.filter_subset_filter` states monotonicity of filter:
    47	if `s ⊆ t` then `s.filter p ⊆ t.filter p`. -/
    48	TheoremDoc Finset.filter_subset_filter as "Finset.filter_subset_filter" in "Finset"
    49	
    50	/-- `Finset.filter_inter_distrib` states that filter distributes
    51	over intersection: `(s ∩ t).filter p = s.filter p ∩ t.filter p`. -/

     7	
     8	Introduction "
     9	# Union Retrieval
    10	
    11	Prove that $(s \\cap t) \\cup u \\subseteq s \\cup u$.
    12	
    13	The intersection $s \\cap t$ is contained in $s$, so anything in
    14	$(s \\cap t) \\cup u$ is either in $s$ (via the intersection) or
    15	in $u$ — both land in $s \\cup u$.
    16	
    17	This combines `subset_iff`, `mem_union`, `mem_inter`, and `cases`.
    18	"
    19	
    20	/-- Intersecting before taking a union can only shrink the first component. -/
    21	Statement (s t u : Finset ℕ) : s ∩ t ∪ u ⊆ s ∪ u := by
    22	  Hint "Unfold the subset: `rw [Finset.subset_iff]` then `intro x hx`."
    23	  rw [Finset.subset_iff]
    24	  intro x hx
    25	  Hint (hidden := true) "Unfold `hx` with `rw [Finset.mem_union] at hx`,
    26	  then `rw [Finset.mem_union]` on the goal and case-split with
    27	  `cases hx with`. In the `inl` case, unfold the intersection to
    28	  extract `x ∈ s`. In the `inr` case, `x ∈ u` maps directly."
    29	  rw [Finset.mem_union] at hx
    30	  rw [Finset.mem_union]
    31	  cases hx with
    32	  | inl h =>
    33	    rw [Finset.mem_inter] at h
    34	    left
    35	    exact h.1
    36	  | inr h =>
    37	    right
    38	    exact h
    39	
    40	Conclusion "
    41	The case split on union membership is the key move. Each branch
    42	routes to the correct side of the target union:
    43	- From $s \\cap t$: extract $x \\in s$, then `left`
    44	- From $u$: directly `right`
    45	"

     1	import Game.Metadata

     6	Title "Union Complement"
     7	
     8	Introduction "
     9	# Subtracting a Component from a Union
    10	
    11	Prove that $(s \\cup t) \\setminus t \\subseteq s$.
    12	
    13	If $x$ is in $s \\cup t$ but not in $t$, then $x$ must have come
    14	from $s$.
    15	
    16	The proof combines `mem_sdiff`, `mem_union`, `cases`, and `by_contra`.
    17	"
    18	
    19	/-- Removing one component from a union leaves a subset of the other. -/
    20	Statement (s t : Finset ℕ) : (s ∪ t) \ t ⊆ s := by
    21	  rw [Finset.subset_iff]
    22	  intro x hx
    23	  Hint "Unfold `hx` with `rw [Finset.mem_sdiff] at hx` then
    24	  `obtain` the parts."
    25	  rw [Finset.mem_sdiff] at hx
    26	  obtain ⟨hut, hnt⟩ := hx
    27	  rw [Finset.mem_union] at hut
    28	  Hint "You have `hut : x ∈ s ∨ x ∈ t` and `hnt : x ∉ t`.
    29	  You can't directly extract `x ∈ s` from the disjunction."
    30	  Hint (hidden := true) "Use `by_contra hns` to assume `x ∉ s`,
    31	  then case-split on `hut`. Both cases give a contradiction:
    32	  `inl` contradicts `hns`, `inr` contradicts `hnt`."
    33	  by_contra hns
    34	  cases hut with
    35	  | inl hxs => exact hns hxs
    36	  | inr hxt => exact hnt hxt
    37	
    38	Conclusion "
    39	The `by_contra` move was key: you couldn't directly extract
    40	`x \\in s` from the disjunction `x \\in s \\lor x \\in t`, but
    41	assuming `x \\notin s` made both branches of the case split
    42	contradictory.
    43	
    44	This is a different use of `by_contra` from the double complement
    45	pattern — here it resolves a disjunction under constraint rather
    46	than extracting positive membership from a double negation.
    47	"
    48	
    49	/-- `sdiff_le_self` states `a \ b ≤ a` in a lattice. -/

    53	TheoremDoc Finset.sdiff_subset as "Finset.sdiff_subset" in "Finset"
    54

    56	TheoremDoc sdiff_le as "sdiff_le" in "Order"

    10

    19	/-- The image of a nonempty finset is nonempty. -/
    20	Statement (s : Finset ℕ) (f : ℕ → ℕ) (h : s.Nonempty) :

    32	Two skills combined:
    33	- **Nonempty extraction**: `obtain ⟨x, hx⟩ := h` pulls out a
    34	  concrete element and its membership proof

    36	  is `x` itself, with `f x = f x` by `rfl`

=== L07_InjectiveNonMembership.lean ===
     1	import Game.Metadata
     2	
     3	World "PsetFinset"
     4	Level 7
     5	
     6	Title "Injective Non-Membership"
     7	
     8	Introduction "
     9	# Injectivity Preserves Non-Membership
    10	
    11	If $f$ is injective and $a \\notin s$, then $f(a) \\notin f(s)$.
    12	
    13	Intuitively: if $a$ isn't in the original set, its image can't
    14	appear in the image set (because no other element maps to $f(a)$
    15	when $f$ is injective).
    16	
    17	The proof uses backward image extraction and injectivity to derive
    18	a contradiction.
    19	"
    20	
    21	/-- Injective functions preserve non-membership. -/
    22	Statement (f : ℕ → ℕ) (hf : Function.Injective f) (s : Finset ℕ)
    23	    (a : ℕ) (hna : a ∉ s) : f a ∉ s.image f := by
    24	  Hint "The goal is `f a ∉ s.image f`, which is a negation.
    25	  Use `intro hfa` to assume `f a ∈ s.image f` and derive `False`."
    26	  intro hfa
    27	  Hint (hidden := true) "Extract the witness: `rw [Finset.mem_image] at hfa`
    28	  then `obtain ⟨b, hbs, hfb⟩ := hfa`. Apply injectivity:
    29	  `have hab : b = a := hf hfb`. Rewrite and contradict:
    30	  `rw [hab] at hbs; exact hna hbs`."
    31	  rw [Finset.mem_image] at hfa
    32	  obtain ⟨b, hbs, hfb⟩ := hfa
    33	  have hab : b = a := hf hfb
    34	  rw [hab] at hbs
    35	  exact hna hbs
    36	
    37	Conclusion "
    38	The proof pattern:
    39	1. Assume $f(a) \\in f(s)$ — extract a witness $b \\in s$ with $f(b) = f(a)$
    40	2. Apply injectivity: $f(b) = f(a) \\implies b = a$
    41	3. Rewrite: $b \\in s$ becomes $a \\in s$, contradicting $a \\notin s$
    42	
    43	This is a different use of injectivity from cardinality preservation —
    44	here injectivity creates a contradiction rather than establishing
    45	a count.
    46	"
    47	
    48	/-- `Finset.not_mem_image_of_injective` states that if `f` is injective
    49	and `a ∉ s`, then `f a ∉ s.image f`. -/
    50	TheoremDoc Finset.not_mem_image_of_injective as "Finset.not_mem_image_of_injective" in "Finset"

     1	import Game.Metadata

     6	Title "Filter Image Containment"
     7	
     8	Introduction "
     9	# Filter + Image Integration
    10	
    11	Prove that filtering a set and then taking its image gives a
    12	subset of the image of the original set:
    13	
    14	$$(s.\\text{filter}\\; p).\\text{image}\\; f \\;\\subseteq\\; s.\\text{image}\\; f$$
    15	
    16	The proof combines backward image extraction, filter unwrapping,
    17	and forward image construction.
    18	"
    19	
    20	/-- Filtering before imaging gives a subset of imaging directly. -/
    21	Statement (s : Finset ℕ) (p : ℕ → Prop) [DecidablePred p] (f : ℕ → ℕ) :
    22	    (s.filter p).image f ⊆ s.image f := by
    23	  rw [Finset.subset_iff]
    24	  intro y hy
    25	  Hint "Extract the witness from `hy` using backward image membership."
    26	  Hint (hidden := true) "`rw [Finset.mem_image] at hy` then
    27	  `obtain ⟨x, hx, hfx⟩ := hy`. Unfold the filter:
    28	  `rw [Finset.mem_filter] at hx`. Build the goal:
    29	  `rw [Finset.mem_image]; exact ⟨x, hx.1, hfx⟩`."
    30	  rw [Finset.mem_image] at hy
    31	  obtain ⟨x, hx, hfx⟩ := hy
    32	  rw [Finset.mem_filter] at hx
    33	  rw [Finset.mem_image]
    34	  exact ⟨x, hx.1, hfx⟩
    35	
    36	Conclusion "
    37	The key insight: the witness `x` works for both sides. From the
    38	filtered image, `x ∈ s.filter p` means `x ∈ s ∧ p x`. The filter
    39	predicate `p x` is discarded — only `x ∈ s` is needed for the
    40	unfiltered image.
    41	
    42	This mirrors L03 (filter preserves containment) but with image
    43	instead of intersection: filtering can only shrink, so the image
    44	of the filtered set is contained in the image of the whole set.
    45	"

    47	/-- `Finset.filter_subset p s` states that `s.filter p ⊆ s`:
    48	filtering can only remove elements, never add them. -/

     1	import Game.Metadata

     6	Title "Image, Union, and Set Difference"
     7	
     8	Introduction "
     9	# Union + Image + Sdiff Integration
    10	
    11	Prove that removing the image of $s$ from the image of $s \\cup t$
    12	leaves only elements from the image of $t$:
    13	
    14	$$f(s \\cup t) \\setminus f(s) \\;\\subseteq\\; f(t)$$
    15	
    16	This integrates moves from FinsetOperations and FinsetImage:
    17	subset, sdiff, union, image backward/forward, cases, and by_contra.
    18	
    19	The interesting case: when the witness came from $s$, you can
    20	build $y \\in f(s)$, which contradicts $y \\notin f(s)$.
    21	"
    22	
    23	/-- Elements in the image of a union but not in the image of s must come from t. -/
    24	Statement (f : ℕ → ℕ) (s t : Finset ℕ) :
    25	    (s ∪ t).image f \ s.image f ⊆ t.image f := by
    26	  rw [Finset.subset_iff]
    27	  intro y hy
    28	  rw [Finset.mem_sdiff] at hy
    29	  obtain ⟨hut, hns⟩ := hy
    30	  Hint "Extract the witness from `hut` using backward image membership,
    31	  then determine whether the witness came from `s` or `t`."
    32	  rw [Finset.mem_image] at hut
    33	  obtain ⟨x, hx, hfx⟩ := hut
    34	  rw [Finset.mem_union] at hx
    35	  Hint (hidden := true) "Case-split on `hx` with `cases hx with`.
    36	  In the `inl` case (`x ∈ s`): use `by_contra` then `apply hns`
    37	  to show `y ∈ s.image f`, contradicting `hns`.
    38	  In the `inr` case (`x ∈ t`): build forward membership directly."
    39	  cases hx with
    40	  | inl hxs =>
    41	    by_contra _
    42	    apply hns
    43	    rw [Finset.mem_image]
    44	    exact ⟨x, hxs, hfx⟩
    45	  | inr hxt =>
    46	    rw [Finset.mem_image]
    47	    exact ⟨x, hxt, hfx⟩
    48	
    49	Conclusion "
    50	This proof integrated 6 moves from two worlds:
    51	
    52	| Move | Source |
    53	|---|---|
    54	| `rw [Finset.subset_iff]; intro y hy` | FinsetOperations |
    55	| `rw [Finset.mem_sdiff] at hy` | FinsetOperations |
    56	| `rw [Finset.mem_image] at hut; obtain ...` | FinsetImage |
    57	| `rw [Finset.mem_union] at hx; cases ...` | FinsetOperations |
    58	| `by_contra; apply hns` | FinsetOperations |
    59	| `rw [Finset.mem_image]; exact ⟨x, ...⟩` | FinsetImage |
    60	
    61	The `inl` case used `by_contra` to expose the contradiction:
    62	if $x \\in s$, then $y = f(x) \\in f(s)$, contradicting $y \\notin f(s)$.
    63	"

     1	import Game.Metadata
     2	
     3	World "PsetFinset"
     4	Level 10
     5	
     6	Title "Boss: Image Preserves Set Difference"
     7	
     8	Introduction "
     9	# Boss: Image and Set Difference Under Injectivity
    10	
    11	Given an **injective** function $f$, prove:
    12	
    13	$$(s \\setminus t).\\text{image}\\; f \\;\\subseteq\\; s.\\text{image}\\; f \\;\\setminus\\; t.\\text{image}\\; f$$
    14	
    15	If you remove elements of $t$ from $s$ *before* applying $f$,
    16	the result is contained in what you'd get by applying $f$ to $s$
    17	and then removing the image of $t$.
    18	
    19	This proof integrates 7+ moves from FinsetOperations and FinsetImage.
    20	No new moves — only moves you've already used.
    21	"
    22	
    23	/-- Under injectivity, image preserves set difference (as a subset). -/
    24	Statement (f : ℕ → ℕ) (hf : Function.Injective f) (s t : Finset ℕ) :
    25	    (s \ t).image f ⊆ s.image f \ t.image f := by
    26	  rw [Finset.subset_iff]
    27	  intro y hy
    28	  Hint "Extract the witness from `hy`, unfold the source sdiff,
    29	  then build both parts of the target sdiff."
    30	  rw [Finset.mem_image] at hy
    31	  obtain ⟨a, ha, hfa⟩ := hy
    32	  rw [Finset.mem_sdiff] at ha
    33	  rw [Finset.mem_sdiff]
    34	  constructor
    35	  -- Part 1: y ∈ s.image f
    36	  · Hint (hidden := true) "`rw [Finset.mem_image]; exact ⟨a, ha.1, hfa⟩`"
    37	    rw [Finset.mem_image]
    38	    exact ⟨a, ha.1, hfa⟩
    39	  -- Part 2: y ∉ t.image f
    40	  · Hint "The goal is `y ∉ t.image f`. Assume `y ∈ t.image f`,
    41	    extract a witness `b ∈ t`, and use injectivity to derive `a = b`."
    42	    Hint (hidden := true) "`intro hyt; rw [Finset.mem_image] at hyt;
    43	    obtain ⟨b, hbt, hfb⟩ := hyt`. Then
    44	    `have hfab : f a = f b := by rw [hfa, hfb]`.
    45	    `have hab : a = b := hf hfab`.
    46	    `rw [hab] at ha; exact ha.2 hbt`."
    47	    intro hyt
    48	    rw [Finset.mem_image] at hyt
    49	    obtain ⟨b, hbt, hfb⟩ := hyt
    50	    have hfab : f a = f b := by rw [hfa, hfb]
    51	    have hab : a = b := hf hfab
    52	    rw [hab] at ha
    53	    exact ha.2 hbt
    54	
    55	Conclusion "
    56	Congratulations — you've completed the **Finset Problem Set**!
    57	
    58	You proved that injective functions preserve set difference:
    59	$f(s \\setminus t) \\subseteq f(s) \\setminus f(t)$ when $f$ is injective.
    60	
    61	The key insight: injectivity was essential. Without it, two
    62	different elements $a \\notin t$ and $b \\in t$ could map to
    63	the same $y$, making $y$ appear in $t.\\text{image}\\; f$ even though
    64	it came from an element outside $t$. Injectivity forces $a = b$,
    65	which creates the contradiction.
    66	
    67	**What's next**: The next world introduces **cardinality** —
    68	the number of elements in a finset — and the fundamental
    69	counting identities.
    70	"
    71	
    72	/-- `Finset.image_sdiff s t hf` states that when `f` is injective,
    73	`(s \\ t).image f = s.image f \\ t.image f` — image distributes
    74	over set difference under injectivity. -/

    77	/-- `Finset.image_sdiff_of_injOn` is the `InjOn` variant of
    78	`Finset.image_sdiff` — requires injectivity only on the relevant sets. -/
    79	TheoremDoc Finset.image_sdiff_of_injOn as "Finset.image_sdiff_of_injOn" in "Finset"
    80

  1406→    69	          exact absurd hlt (by omega)
  1407→    70	
  1408→    71	Conclusion "
  1409→    72	You've now done case analysis on both `Fin 2` (Level 17) and `Fin 3`.
  1410→    73	
  1411→    74	The pattern scales: for `Fin n`, you get `n` valid cases and one
  1412→    75	impossible case. Each additional `n` adds one more nesting level.
  1413→    76	
  1414→    77	For large `n`, this would be tedious. That's why the `fin_cases`
  1415→    78	tactic (currently disabled) exists — it automates the entire case
  1416→    79	split. You'll unlock it in a later world.
  1417→    80	
  1418→    81	You've now verified by case analysis that `Fin 0` has 0 elements,
  1419→    82	`Fin 1` has 1, `Fin 2` has 2, and `Fin 3` has 3. The general fact —
  1420→    83	`Fin n` has exactly `n` elements — will be formalized using
  1421→    84	`Fintype.card` in the Cardinality world.
  1422→    85	
  1423→    86	This practice prepares you for the boss, where you'll combine case
  1424→    87	analysis with other moves.
  1425→    88	"
  1426→    89	
  1427→    90	DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases tauto
  1428→    91	DisabledTheorem Fin.forall_fin_two

  1430→=== L19_ModularArithmetic.lean ===
  1431→     1	import Game.Metadata
  1432→     2	
  1433→     3	World "MeetFin"
  1434→     4	Level 19
  1435→     5	
  1436→     6	Title "Modular Arithmetic"
  1437→     7	
  1438→     8	Introduction "
  1439→     9	# Enrichment: Fin Has Arithmetic
  1440→    10	
  1441→    11	*This level is an enrichment aside — a mathematical surprise that
  1442→    12	connects `Fin n` to a different area of mathematics. The main course
  1443→    13	path resumes in the next level.*
  1444→    14	
  1445→    15	You've been treating `Fin n` as an index set — a collection of
  1446→    16	positions `0, 1, ..., n-1`. But `Fin n` also supports **arithmetic**,
  1447→    17	and it works differently from natural number arithmetic.
  1448→    18	
  1449→    19	What is `3 + 4` in `Fin 5`? On natural numbers, it's `7`. But `Fin 5`
  1450→    20	only has elements `0, 1, 2, 3, 4` — there is no `7`. Addition on
  1451→    21	`Fin n` **wraps around**: it takes the result modulo `n`.
  1452→    22	
  1453→    23	So `(3 : Fin 5) + (4 : Fin 5) = (2 : Fin 5)` because `7 mod 5 = 2`.
  1454→    24	This is **modular arithmetic** — the arithmetic of clocks, cyclic
  1455→    25	groups, and the integers mod `n`.
  1456→    26	
  1457→    27	**Your task**: Find two elements `a, b : Fin 5` such that
  1458→    28	`a + b = 0` (their sum wraps around to zero) and their natural
  1459→    29	number values sum to `5`.
  1460→    30	
  1461→    31	(Hint: on a 5-hour clock, which two different hours are diametrically
  1462→    32	opposite — summing to a full rotation?)
  1463→    33	"
  1464→    34	
  1465→    35	/-- Find two Fin 5 elements that sum to 0 modularly and to 5 naturally. -/
  1466→    36	Statement : ∃ a b : Fin 5, a + b = 0 ∧ a.val + b.val = 5 := by
  1467→    37	  Hint "Provide both witnesses with `use`.
  1468→    38	  Which two numbers less than 5 sum to 5 as naturals and to 0 mod 5?"
  1469→    39	  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `use ⟨1, by omega⟩, ⟨4, by omega⟩`."
  1470→    40	  use ⟨1, by omega⟩, ⟨4, by omega⟩
  1471→    41	  Hint "Now prove both parts of the conjunction with `constructor`."
  1472→    42	  constructor
  1473→    43	  · Hint "The sum `1 + 4` in Fin 5 equals `5 mod 5 = 0`.
  1474→    44	    Since this is a definitional computation, `rfl` closes it."
  1475→    45	    rfl
  1476→    46	  · Hint "The natural sum `1 + 4 = 5`. Since the values are concrete
  1477→    47	    numbers, Lean computes the result and `rfl` closes it."
  1478→    48	    rfl
  1479→    49	
  1480→    50	Conclusion "
  1481→    51	Every nonzero element of `Fin 5` generates the whole group under
  1482→    52	repeated addition — after exactly 5 steps, you return to 0. This
  1483→    53	is because 5 is prime, making `Fin 5` a **cyclic group** where
  1484→    54	every nonzero element is a generator.
  1485→    55	
  1486→    56	More examples of modular arithmetic in `Fin 5`:
  1487→    57	- `(3 : Fin 5) + (4 : Fin 5) = 2` (because `7 mod 5 = 2`)
  1488→    58	- `(2 : Fin 5) * (3 : Fin 5) = 1` (because `6 mod 5 = 1`)
  1489→    59	- `(4 : Fin 5) + (1 : Fin 5) = 0` (because `5 mod 5 = 0`)
  1490→    60	
  1491→    61	Notice that both halves of the conjunction close by `rfl` — both
  1492→    62	are *definitional computations*. The modular sum `1 + 4` reduces
  1493→    63	to `0` in `Fin 5` because Lean computes `5 mod 5 = 0`. The natural
  1494→    64	sum `1 + 4` reduces to `5` because Lean computes `1 + 4 = 5`.
  1495→    65	The `constructor` tactic split the conjunction so each half could
  1496→    66	be verified independently.
  1497→    67	
  1498→    68	This is the arithmetic of a **clock with `n` hours**: when you pass
  1499→    69	the maximum, you wrap back to 0. Mathematically, `Fin n` with this
  1500→    70	arithmetic is a model of the integers modulo `n` (written
  1501→    71	$\\mathbb{Z}/n\\mathbb{Z}$).
  1502→    72	
  1503→    73	This course focuses on `Fin n` as a finite index type, not as a
  1504→    74	number system. But this modular structure appears again sooner than
  1505→    75	you might expect. In the Problem Set (PsetFin), you'll study the
  1506→    76	cyclic permutation `![1, 2, 0] : Fin 3 -> Fin 3`. That permutation
  1507→    77	cycles `0 -> 1 -> 2 -> 0` — exactly the same cyclic structure you
  1508→    78	see here with addition modulo 5. The cyclic permutation and
  1509→    79	modular addition are both examples of **cyclic groups**: structures
  1510→    80	where repeated application of an operation eventually returns to
  1511→    81	the starting point.
  1512→    82	
  1513→    83	When you study `Fintype.card` in the Cardinality world, the fact
  1514→    84	that `Fintype.card (Fin n) = n` connects the type-theoretic size
  1515→    85	of `Fin n` to this algebraic structure.
  1516→    86	
  1517→    87	Now back to our main path: using `Fin` as an index for concrete data.
  1518→    88	"
  1519→    89	
  1520→    90	DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases tauto
  1521→    91	DisabledTheorem Fin.forall_fin_two

  1523→=== L20_ConcreteApplication.lean ===
  1524→     1	import Game.Metadata
  1525→     2	
  1526→     3	World "MeetFin"
  1527→     4	Level 20
  1528→     5	
  1529→     6	Title "Using Fin as an Index"
  1530→     7	
  1531→     8	Introduction "
  1532→     9	# Fin in Action: Indexing Concrete Data
  1533→    10	
  1534→    11	So far, `Fin` has been an abstract mathematical object. But `Fin n`
  1535→    12	exists to **index things** — vector entries, polygon vertices, tuple
  1536→    13	positions.
  1537→    14	
  1538→    15	A function `Fin n → α` is essentially an `n`-element tuple: it assigns
  1539→    16	a value of type `α` to each index `0, 1, ..., n-1`. You'll meet tuples
  1540→    17	formally in the FinTuples world, but let's preview the idea.
  1541→    18	
  1542→    19	**Key fact**: A function on `Fin 3` is completely determined by its
  1543→    20	three values — `f 0`, `f 1`, and `f 2`. If two functions agree on
  1544→    21	these three inputs, they agree everywhere. This is because `Fin 3`
  1545→    22	has exactly three elements (as you proved in Level 18).
  1546→    23	
  1547→    24	**Your task**: Prove that two functions `f g : Fin 3 → ℕ` are
  1548→    25	equal on all inputs if they agree at positions 0, 1, and 2.
  1549→    26	
  1550→    27	This requires case analysis: for each possible input `i : Fin 3`,
  1551→    28	determine which of the three hypotheses closes the goal.
  1552→    29	"
  1553→    30	
  1554→    31	/-- A function on Fin 3 is determined by its three values. -/
  1555→    32	Statement (f g : Fin 3 → ℕ) (h0 : f 0 = g 0) (h1 : f 1 = g 1)
  1556→    33	    (h2 : f 2 = g 2) : ∀ i : Fin 3, f i = g i := by
  1557→    34	  Hint "Start by introducing `i`, then destructure it:
  1558→    35	  `intro i; cases i with | mk v hlt =>`"
  1559→    36	  intro i
  1560→    37	  Hint (hidden := true) "`cases i with | mk v hlt =>`"
  1561→    38	  cases i with
  1562→    39	  | mk v hlt =>
  1563→    40	    Hint "Case-split on `v` to determine which position `i` is at.
  1564→    41	    For each valid case (0, 1, 2), one of the three hypotheses
  1565→    42	    closes the goal."
  1566→    43	    cases v with
  1567→    44	    | zero =>
  1568→    45	      Hint "Here `i` is at position 0 (value `v = 0`).
  1569→    46	      One of the three hypotheses `h0`, `h1`, `h2` matches.
  1570→    47	      Use `exact h0`."
  1571→    48	      exact h0
  1572→    49	    | succ n =>
  1573→    50	      Hint (hidden := true) "Case-split on `n`."
  1574→    51	      cases n with
  1575→    52	      | zero =>
  1576→    53	        Hint (hidden := true) "Here `i` is at position 1. `exact h1` closes the goal."
  1577→    54	        exact h1
  1578→    55	      | succ m =>
  1579→    56	        Hint (hidden := true) "Case-split on `m`."
  1580→    57	        cases m with
  1581→    58	        | zero =>
  1582→    59	          Hint (hidden := true) "Here `i` is at position 2. `exact h2` closes the goal."
  1583→    60	          exact h2
  1584→    61	        | succ k =>
  1585→    62	          Hint (hidden := true) "Impossible case: `exact absurd hlt (by omega)`."
  1586→    63	          exact absurd hlt (by omega)
  1587→    64	
  1588→    65	Conclusion "
  1589→    66	This is the fundamental property of `Fin n` as an index type: since
  1590→    67	`Fin n` has exactly `n` elements, any predicate on `Fin n` can be
  1591→    68	verified by checking each element. A function `Fin n → α` is
  1592→    69	determined by `n` values, and two such functions agree if and only
  1593→    70	if they agree on all `n` inputs.
  1594→    71	
  1595→    72	This is how `Fin n` connects to the concept of an **n-tuple**: the
  1596→    73	function `f : Fin 3 → ℕ` is just the triple `(f 0, f 1, f 2)`. Two
  1597→    74	triples are equal when they agree position by position. You'll
  1598→    75	formalize this connection in the FinTuples world.
  1599→    76	
  1600→    77	In the next level, you'll learn to prove that two `Fin` elements
  1601→    78	are *not* equal — the inequality pattern.
  1602→    79	"
  1603→    80	
  1604→    81	DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases tauto
  1605→    82	DisabledTheorem Fin.forall_fin_two

  1607→=== L21_ProvingInequality.lean ===
  1608→     1	import Game.Metadata
  1609→     2	
  1610→     3	World "MeetFin"
  1611→     4	Level 21
  1612→     5	
  1613→     6	Title "Proving Inequality"
  1614→     7	
  1615→     8	Introduction "
  1616→     9	# Proving Two Fin Elements Are Not Equal
  1617→    10	
  1618→    11	In Level 17, you used `absurd` when a bound was violated — the value
  1619→    12	was too large but the bound said it must be small. Here the situation
  1620→    13	is different: the *constructors themselves* don't match (like `0 = 1`),
  1621→    14	and a simpler pattern handles this case.
  1622→    15	
  1623→    16	How do you prove `a ≠ b` for `Fin` elements? In Lean, `a ≠ b` is
  1624→    17	defined as `a = b → False` — a function that takes an equality proof
  1625→    18	and derives a contradiction.
  1626→    19	
  1627→    20	The strategy is:
  1628→    21	1. `intro h` — assume `a = b` (giving `h : a = b`)
  1629→    22	2. `cases h` — Lean checks if the constructors match; if the underlying
  1630→    23	   values differ, it closes the goal automatically
  1631→    24	
  1632→    25	When `h : ⟨a, _⟩ = ⟨b, _⟩` and `a ≠ b` as natural numbers, `cases h`
  1633→    26	performs *injection* — it sees the values inside the constructors don't
  1634→    27	match and derives a contradiction. This is the same idea as `Fin.ext_iff`
  1635→    28	in reverse: if the values differ, the elements can't be equal.
  1636→    29	
  1637→    30	**Your task**: Prove that `0` and `1` are different elements of `Fin 3`.
  1638→    31	"
  1639→    32	
  1640→    33	/-- `0` and `1` are distinct elements of `Fin 3`. -/
  1641→    34	Statement : (0 : Fin 3) ≠ 1 := by
  1642→    35	  Hint "Remember, `≠` means `= → False`. Start with `intro h`
  1643→    36	  to assume they are equal."
  1644→    37	  intro h
  1645→    38	  Hint "Now `{h}` says these `Fin` elements are equal. But their
  1646→    39	  underlying values are `0` and `1` — different natural numbers.
  1647→    40	  `cases h` performs injection and sees the contradiction."
  1648→    41	  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `cases h`."
  1649→    42	  cases h
  1650→    43	
  1651→    44	Conclusion "
  1652→    45	The pattern for proving `a ≠ b` on `Fin` elements is:
  1653→    46	```
  1654→    47	intro h
  1655→    48	cases h
  1656→    49	```
  1657→    50	
  1658→    51	This is called **constructor discrimination**: when `cases` examines
  1659→    52	an equality `h : ⟨a, _⟩ = ⟨b, _⟩`, it performs *injection* (extracts
  1660→    53	that `a = b`) and then checks whether this is consistent. When `a`
  1661→    54	and `b` are distinct constructors (like `Nat.zero` and `Nat.succ _`),
  1662→    55	the equality is impossible, and the goal closes automatically. This
  1663→    56	pattern works for any type with distinct constructors — `Nat`, `Bool`,
  1664→    57	`Fin`, etc. — not just `Fin`.
  1665→    58	
  1666→    59	You could also prove this using `Fin.ext_iff`: `intro h; rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h; omega`.
  1667→    60	This reduces the `Fin` equality to `0 = 1` at the `ℕ` level, then
  1668→    61	`omega` sees the contradiction. Both approaches work — here's when
  1669→    62	to choose each:
  1670→    63	
  1671→    64	- **`intro h; cases h`** — use when both sides are **concrete literals**
  1672→    65	  (like `0` and `1`). The constructor discrimination is automatic.
  1673→    66	- **`intro h; rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h; omega`** — use when either side
  1674→    67	  involves a **variable** or **arithmetic expression** (like `i` or
  1675→    68	  `i + 1`). The `cases` approach won't fire on variable expressions,
  1676→    69	  but reducing to values and using `omega` always works.
  1677→    70	
  1678→    71	This is the discrimination step you'll need in the boss.
  1679→    72	
  1680→    73	**Looking ahead**: The fact that Lean can *computationally* determine
  1681→    74	whether two `Fin` elements are equal is called **decidable equality**.
  1682→    75	The `intro h; cases h` pattern works because Lean's kernel can evaluate
  1683→    76	whether the underlying constructors match. This property — `DecidableEq
  1684→    77	(Fin n)` — is what will make `Finset (Fin n)` possible in Phase 2: a
  1685→    78	finite set needs to check for duplicates, and decidable equality is what
  1686→    79	makes that check computable.
  1687→    80	"
  1688→    81	
  1689→    82	DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases tauto
  1690→
  1691→=== L22_ExistentialWitness.lean ===
  1692→     1	import Game.Metadata
  1693→     2	
  1694→     3	World "MeetFin"
  1695→     4	Level 22
  1696→     5	
  1697→     6	Title "Existential Witnesses"
  1698→     7	
  1699→     8	Introduction "
  1700→     9	# Providing a Fin Element as a Witness
  1701→    10	
  1702→    11	In Level 1, you constructed a `Fin` element with `exact ⟨k, by omega⟩`.
  1703→    12	But what if the goal asks you to prove that *some* element exists
  1704→    13	with a property? Then you need `use` to provide the witness.
  1705→    14	
  1706→    15	When the goal is `∃ x : Fin n, P x`, the tactic `use ⟨k, by omega⟩`
  1707→    16	provides a specific `Fin n` element as the witness, then asks you
  1708→    17	to prove `P` holds for that element.
  1709→    18	
  1710→    19	This combines two skills you already know:
  1711→    20	- `use` from the NNG4 prerequisites (providing existential witnesses)
  1712→    21	- `⟨k, by omega⟩` from Level 1 (constructing `Fin` elements)
  1713→    22	
  1714→    23	**Your task**: Find a `Fin 5` element whose value is `3`.
  1715→    24	"
  1716→    25	
  1717→    26	/-- There exists an element of `Fin 5` with value `3`. -/
  1718→    27	Statement : ∃ x : Fin 5, x.val = 3 := by
  1719→    28	  Hint "Provide the witness with `use ⟨3, by omega⟩`. This constructs
  1720→    29	  the element `3 : Fin 5` and provides it as the existential witness.
  1721→    30	  The remaining goal `3 = 3` closes automatically."
  1722→    31	  Hint (hidden := true) "`use ⟨3, by omega⟩` provides the witness and
  1723→    32	  Lean closes `3 = 3` by reflexivity."
  1724→    33	  use ⟨3, by omega⟩
  1725→    34	
  1726→    35	Conclusion "
  1727→    36	The pattern `use ⟨k, by omega⟩` provides a `Fin` element as an
  1728→    37	existential witness. This is simply the combination of two moves:
  1729→    38	
  1730→    39	| Move | From |
  1731→    40	|---|---|
  1732→    41	| `use expr` — provide a witness | NNG4 prerequisites |
  1733→    42	| `⟨k, by omega⟩` — construct a Fin element | Level 1 |
  1734→    43	
  1735→    44	You'll use this combination in the boss.
  1736→    45	"
  1737→    46	
  1738→    47	DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases tauto
  1739→
  1740→=== L23_Boss.lean ===
  1741→     1	import Game.Metadata
  1742→     2	
  1743→     3	World "MeetFin"
  1744→     4	Level 23
  1745→     5	
  1746→     6	Title "Boss: Fin Fundamentals"
  1747→     7	
  1748→     8	Introduction "
  1749→     9	# Boss: Putting It All Together
  1750→    10	
  1751→    11	Time to combine everything you've learned in a two-part challenge.
  1752→    12	
  1753→    13	**Part 1** — *Transitivity through extensionality*: If `x` and `y`
  1754→    14	have the same value, and `y = z` as Fin elements, then `x = z`. This
  1755→    15	combines `ext` on the goal (Level 15) with `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h`
  1756→    16	on hypotheses (Level 7).
  1757→    17	
  1758→    18	**Part 2** — *Non-triviality*: Fin 3 has more than one element.
  1759→    19	This requires exhaustive case analysis (Levels 16–18), existential
  1760→    20	witnesses (Level 22), inequality proving (Level 21), and the Fin
  1761→    21	contradiction pattern (Level 13) for the impossible branch.
  1762→    22	
  1763→    23	The boss tests whether you can assemble these moves into a
  1764→    24	coherent proof. No single level taught all of this at once.
  1765→    25	"
  1766→    26	
  1767→    27	/-- Fin equality is transitive through values, and Fin 3 is non-trivial. -/
  1768→    28	Statement : (∀ x y z : Fin 3, x.val = y.val → y = z → x = z) ∧
  1769→    29	            (∀ x : Fin 3, ∃ y : Fin 3, x ≠ y) := by
  1770→    30	  Hint "The goal is a conjunction. Use `constructor` to split it
  1771→    31	  into two sub-goals."
  1772→    32	  constructor
  1773→    33	  -- Part 1: transitivity through extensionality
  1774→    34	  · Hint "Start with `intro x y z h1 h2`."
  1775→    35	    intro x y z h1 h2
  1776→    36	    Hint "You need to show `x = z`. Use `ext` to convert this to
  1777→    37	    a value equality `x.val = z.val`."
  1778→    38	    ext
  1779→    39	    Hint "Now you need `x.val = z.val`. Use `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h2`
  1780→    40	    to extract `y.val = z.val` from `{h2}`."
  1781→    41	    rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h2
  1782→    42	    Hint "Now `{h1}` says `x.val = y.val` and `{h2}` says
  1783→    43	    `y.val = z.val`. `omega` can chain these."
  1784→    44	    omega
  1785→    45	  -- Part 2: non-triviality via exhaustive case analysis
  1786→    46	  · Hint "Perform exhaustive case analysis: destructure `x` with
  1787→    47	    `cases`, then split on the value. For each valid case (0, 1, 2),
  1788→    48	    choose a different `Fin 3` element as witness and prove inequality
  1789→    49	    with `intro h; cases h`. The impossible case (value >= 3) closes
  1790→    50	    with `absurd`."
  1791→    51	    intro x
  1792→    52	    Hint (hidden := true) "Destructure `x`: `cases x with | mk v hv =>`"
  1793→    53	    cases x with
  1794→    54	    | mk v hv =>
  1795→    55	      Hint (hidden := true) "Case-split on `v`:
  1796→    56	      `cases v with | zero => ... | succ n => ...`"
  1797→    57	      cases v with
  1798→    58	      | zero =>
  1799→    59	        Hint (hidden := true) "The element is `0`. Provide `1` as witness:
  1800→    60	        `use ⟨1, by omega⟩`, then prove `0 ≠ 1` with `intro h; cases h`."
  1801→    61	        use ⟨1, by omega⟩
  1802→    62	        intro h
  1803→    63	        cases h
  1804→    64	      | succ n =>
  1805→    65	        Hint (hidden := true) "The element has value `n + 1`. Split on `n`."
  1806→    66	        cases n with
  1807→    67	        | zero =>
  1808→    68	          Hint (hidden := true) "The element is `1`. Use `⟨0, by omega⟩`
  1809→    69	          as witness, then `intro h; cases h`."
  1810→    70	          use ⟨0, by omega⟩
  1811→    71	          intro h
  1812→    72	          cases h
  1813→    73	        | succ m =>
  1814→    74	          Hint (hidden := true) "The element has value `m + 2`. Split on `m`."
  1815→    75	          cases m with
  1816→    76	          | zero =>
  1817→    77	            Hint (hidden := true) "The element is `2`. Use `⟨0, by omega⟩`
  1818→    78	            as witness, then `intro h; cases h`."
  1819→    79	            use ⟨0, by omega⟩
  1820→    80	            intro h
  1821→    81	            cases h
  1822→    82	          | succ k =>
  1823→    83	            Hint (hidden := true) "Here `v = k + 3` but `hv` says `v < 3`.
  1824→    84	            This is impossible: `exact absurd hv (by omega)`."
  1825→    85	            exact absurd hv (by omega)
  1826→    86	
  1827→    87	Conclusion "
  1828→    88	Congratulations — you've completed **Meet Fin**!
  1829→    89	
  1830→    90	You now have these core moves for working with `Fin n`:
  1831→    91	
  1832→    92	| Move | Tool | Learned in |
  1833→    93	|---|---|---|
  1834→    94	| Construct a `Fin n` element | `⟨k, by omega⟩` | Level 1 |
  1835→    95	| Extract the number / coercion | `.val`, `.isLt`, `↑i` | Level 2 |
  1836→    96	| Upper bound proof | `.isLt` + `omega` | Level 3 |
  1837→    97	| Combine bounds from multiple elements | multiple `.isLt` + `omega` | Level 4 |
  1838→    98	| Prove `Fin` elements equal | `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` or `ext` | Levels 5, 15 |
  1839→    99	| Rewrite hypotheses | `rw [...] at h` | Levels 7–8 |
  1840→   100	| Derive Fin.eta | `rw [Fin.ext_iff]; rfl` | Level 10 |
  1841→   101	| Proof irrelevance | `Fin.ext_iff` + `omega` | Level 11 |
  1842→   102	| General `Fin (n+1)` reasoning | `.isLt` + `omega` | Level 12 |
  1843→   103	| Fin contradiction pattern | `.isLt` + `omega` on empty/impossible | Level 13 |
  1844→   104	| Handle `Fin 1` (singleton) | `ext` + `omega` | Level 15 |
  1845→   105	| Destructure and case-split | `cases` (nested) + `absurd` | Levels 16–18 |
  1846→   106	| Modular arithmetic | construction + `rfl` | Level 19 |
  1847→   107	| Use Fin as a concrete index | case analysis on indexed data | Level 20 |
  1848→   108	| Prove `Fin` inequality | `intro h; cases h` | Level 21 |
  1849→   109	| Existential witness | `use ⟨k, by omega⟩` | Level 22 |
  1850→   110	
  1851→   111	**Where this is going**: `Fin n` is the *canonical* finite type in
  1852→   112	Lean — the standard model that everything connects back to. Every
  1853→   113	finite type with `n` elements is equivalent to `Fin n`, making it
  1854→   114	the universal representative. A function `Fin n → α` is essentially
  1855→   115	an `n`-tuple: an ordered list of `n` elements from `α`. You'll
  1856→   116	explore this idea in the FinTuples world.
  1857→   117	
  1858→   118	Beyond tuples, you'll meet `Finset` (unordered finite collections),
  1859→   119	`Fintype` (types with finitely many elements), and big operators
  1860→   120	like `∑` and `∏` over finite index sets. All of these are built on
  1861→   121	the foundation you've just learned.
  1862→   122	
  1863→   123	(As you saw in Level 19, `Fin n` also carries modular arithmetic —
  1864→   124	addition and multiplication wrap around mod `n`, making `Fin n` a
  1865→   125	model of `ℤ/nℤ`. This course focuses on `Fin` as a finite index
  1866→   126	type, not as a number system.)
  1867→   127	
  1868→   128	In the next world, you'll learn to **navigate** within `Fin n` —
  1869→   129	moving forward with `Fin.succ`, embedding with `Fin.castSucc`, and
  1870→   130	reaching the boundary with `Fin.last`.
  1871→   131	"

=== L19_ModularArithmetic.lean ===
     1	import Game.Metadata
     2	
     3	World "MeetFin"
     4	Level 19
     5	
     6	Title "Modular Arithmetic"
     7	
     8	Introduction "
     9	# Enrichment: Fin Has Arithmetic
    10	
    11	*This level is an enrichment aside — a mathematical surprise that
    12	connects `Fin n` to a different area of mathematics. The main course
    13	path resumes in the next level.*
    14	
    15	You've been treating `Fin n` as an index set — a collection of
    16	positions `0, 1, ..., n-1`. But `Fin n` also supports **arithmetic**,
    17	and it works differently from natural number arithmetic.
    18	
    19	What is `3 + 4` in `Fin 5`? On natural numbers, it's `7`. But `Fin 5`
    20	only has elements `0, 1, 2, 3, 4` — there is no `7`. Addition on
    21	`Fin n` **wraps around**: it takes the result modulo `n`.
    22	
    23	So `(3 : Fin 5) + (4 : Fin 5) = (2 : Fin 5)` because `7 mod 5 = 2`.
    24	This is **modular arithmetic** — the arithmetic of clocks, cyclic
    25	groups, and the integers mod `n`.
    26	
    27	**Your task**: Find two elements `a, b : Fin 5` such that
    28	`a + b = 0` (their sum wraps around to zero) and their natural
    29	number values sum to `5`.
    30	
    31	(Hint: on a 5-hour clock, which two different hours are diametrically
    32	opposite — summing to a full rotation?)
    33	"
    34	
    35	/-- Find two Fin 5 elements that sum to 0 modularly and to 5 naturally. -/
    36	Statement : ∃ a b : Fin 5, a + b = 0 ∧ a.val + b.val = 5 := by
    37	  Hint "Provide both witnesses with `use`.
    38	  Which two numbers less than 5 sum to 5 as naturals and to 0 mod 5?"
    39	  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `use ⟨1, by omega⟩, ⟨4, by omega⟩`."
    40	  use ⟨1, by omega⟩, ⟨4, by omega⟩
    41	  Hint "Now prove both parts of the conjunction with `constructor`."
    42	  constructor
    43	  · Hint "The sum `1 + 4` in Fin 5 equals `5 mod 5 = 0`.
    44	    Since this is a definitional computation, `rfl` closes it."
    45	    rfl
    46	  · Hint "The natural sum `1 + 4 = 5`. Since the values are concrete
    47	    numbers, Lean computes the result and `rfl` closes it."
    48	    rfl
    49	
    50	Conclusion "
    51	Every nonzero element of `Fin 5` generates the whole group under
    52	repeated addition — after exactly 5 steps, you return to 0. This
    53	is because 5 is prime, making `Fin 5` a **cyclic group** where
    54	every nonzero element is a generator.
    55	
    56	More examples of modular arithmetic in `Fin 5`:
    57	- `(3 : Fin 5) + (4 : Fin 5) = 2` (because `7 mod 5 = 2`)
    58	- `(2 : Fin 5) * (3 : Fin 5) = 1` (because `6 mod 5 = 1`)
    59	- `(4 : Fin 5) + (1 : Fin 5) = 0` (because `5 mod 5 = 0`)
    60	
    61	Notice that both halves of the conjunction close by `rfl` — both
    62	are *definitional computations*. The modular sum `1 + 4` reduces
    63	to `0` in `Fin 5` because Lean computes `5 mod 5 = 0`. The natural
    64	sum `1 + 4` reduces to `5` because Lean computes `1 + 4 = 5`.
    65	The `constructor` tactic split the conjunction so each half could
    66	be verified independently.
    67	
    68	This is the arithmetic of a **clock with `n` hours**: when you pass
    69	the maximum, you wrap back to 0. Mathematically, `Fin n` with this
    70	arithmetic is a model of the integers modulo `n` (written
    71	$\\mathbb{Z}/n\\mathbb{Z}$).
    72	
    73	This course focuses on `Fin n` as a finite index type, not as a
    74	number system. But this modular structure appears again sooner than
    75	you might expect. In the Problem Set (PsetFin), you'll study the
    76	cyclic permutation `![1, 2, 0] : Fin 3 -> Fin 3`. That permutation
    77	cycles `0 -> 1 -> 2 -> 0` — exactly the same cyclic structure you
    78	see here with addition modulo 5. The cyclic permutation and
    79	modular addition are both examples of **cyclic groups**: structures
    80	where repeated application of an operation eventually returns to
    81	the starting point.
    82	
    83	When you study `Fintype.card` in the Cardinality world, the fact
    84	that `Fintype.card (Fin n) = n` connects the type-theoretic size
    85	of `Fin n` to this algebraic structure.
    86	
    87	Now back to our main path: using `Fin` as an index for concrete data.
    88	"
    89	
    90	DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases tauto
    91	DisabledTheorem Fin.forall_fin_two

=== L20_ConcreteApplication.lean ===
     1	import Game.Metadata
     2	
     3	World "MeetFin"
     4	Level 20
     5	
     6	Title "Using Fin as an Index"
     7	
     8	Introduction "
     9	# Fin in Action: Indexing Concrete Data
    10	
    11	So far, `Fin` has been an abstract mathematical object. But `Fin n`
    12	exists to **index things** — vector entries, polygon vertices, tuple
    13	positions.
    14	
    15	A function `Fin n → α` is essentially an `n`-element tuple: it assigns
    16	a value of type `α` to each index `0, 1, ..., n-1`. You'll meet tuples
    17	formally in the FinTuples world, but let's preview the idea.
    18	
    19	**Key fact**: A function on `Fin 3` is completely determined by its
    20	three values — `f 0`, `f 1`, and `f 2`. If two functions agree on
    21	these three inputs, they agree everywhere. This is because `Fin 3`
    22	has exactly three elements (as you proved in Level 18).
    23	
    24	**Your task**: Prove that two functions `f g : Fin 3 → ℕ` are
    25	equal on all inputs if they agree at positions 0, 1, and 2.
    26	
    27	This requires case analysis: for each possible input `i : Fin 3`,
    28	determine which of the three hypotheses closes the goal.
    29	"
    30	
    31	/-- A function on Fin 3 is determined by its three values. -/
    32	Statement (f g : Fin 3 → ℕ) (h0 : f 0 = g 0) (h1 : f 1 = g 1)
    33	    (h2 : f 2 = g 2) : ∀ i : Fin 3, f i = g i := by
    34	  Hint "Start by introducing `i`, then destructure it:
    35	  `intro i; cases i with | mk v hlt =>`"
    36	  intro i
    37	  Hint (hidden := true) "`cases i with | mk v hlt =>`"
    38	  cases i with
    39	  | mk v hlt =>
    40	    Hint "Case-split on `v` to determine which position `i` is at.
    41	    For each valid case (0, 1, 2), one of the three hypotheses
    42	    closes the goal."
    43	    cases v with
    44	    | zero =>
    45	      Hint "Here `i` is at position 0 (value `v = 0`).
    46	      One of the three hypotheses `h0`, `h1`, `h2` matches.
    47	      Use `exact h0`."
    48	      exact h0
    49	    | succ n =>
    50	      Hint (hidden := true) "Case-split on `n`."
    51	      cases n with
    52	      | zero =>
    53	        Hint (hidden := true) "Here `i` is at position 1. `exact h1` closes the goal."
    54	        exact h1
    55	      | succ m =>
    56	        Hint (hidden := true) "Case-split on `m`."
    57	        cases m with
    58	        | zero =>
    59	          Hint (hidden := true) "Here `i` is at position 2. `exact h2` closes the goal."
    60	          exact h2
    61	        | succ k =>
    62	          Hint (hidden := true) "Impossible case: `exact absurd hlt (by omega)`."
    63	          exact absurd hlt (by omega)
    64	
    65	Conclusion "
    66	This is the fundamental property of `Fin n` as an index type: since
    67	`Fin n` has exactly `n` elements, any predicate on `Fin n` can be
    68	verified by checking each element. A function `Fin n → α` is
    69	determined by `n` values, and two such functions agree if and only
    70	if they agree on all `n` inputs.
    71	
    72	This is how `Fin n` connects to the concept of an **n-tuple**: the
    73	function `f : Fin 3 → ℕ` is just the triple `(f 0, f 1, f 2)`. Two
    74	triples are equal when they agree position by position. You'll
    75	formalize this connection in the FinTuples world.
    76	
    77	In the next level, you'll learn to prove that two `Fin` elements
    78	are *not* equal — the inequality pattern.
    79	"
    80	
    81	DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases tauto
    82	DisabledTheorem Fin.forall_fin_two

=== L21_ProvingInequality.lean ===
     1	import Game.Metadata
     2	
     3	World "MeetFin"
     4	Level 21
     5	
     6	Title "Proving Inequality"
     7	
     8	Introduction "
     9	# Proving Two Fin Elements Are Not Equal
    10	
    11	In Level 17, you used `absurd` when a bound was violated — the value
    12	was too large but the bound said it must be small. Here the situation
    13	is different: the *constructors themselves* don't match (like `0 = 1`),
    14	and a simpler pattern handles this case.
    15	
    16	How do you prove `a ≠ b` for `Fin` elements? In Lean, `a ≠ b` is
    17	defined as `a = b → False` — a function that takes an equality proof
    18	and derives a contradiction.
    19	
    20	The strategy is:
    21	1. `intro h` — assume `a = b` (giving `h : a = b`)
    22	2. `cases h` — Lean checks if the constructors match; if the underlying
    23	   values differ, it closes the goal automatically
    24	
    25	When `h : ⟨a, _⟩ = ⟨b, _⟩` and `a ≠ b` as natural numbers, `cases h`
    26	performs *injection* — it sees the values inside the constructors don't
    27	match and derives a contradiction. This is the same idea as `Fin.ext_iff`
    28	in reverse: if the values differ, the elements can't be equal.
    29	
    30	**Your task**: Prove that `0` and `1` are different elements of `Fin 3`.
    31	"
    32	
    33	/-- `0` and `1` are distinct elements of `Fin 3`. -/
    34	Statement : (0 : Fin 3) ≠ 1 := by
    35	  Hint "Remember, `≠` means `= → False`. Start with `intro h`
    36	  to assume they are equal."
    37	  intro h
    38	  Hint "Now `{h}` says these `Fin` elements are equal. But their
    39	  underlying values are `0` and `1` — different natural numbers.
    40	  `cases h` performs injection and sees the contradiction."
    41	  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `cases h`."
    42	  cases h
    43	
    44	Conclusion "
    45	The pattern for proving `a ≠ b` on `Fin` elements is:
    46	```
    47	intro h
    48	cases h
    49	```
    50	
    51	This is called **constructor discrimination**: when `cases` examines
    52	an equality `h : ⟨a, _⟩ = ⟨b, _⟩`, it performs *injection* (extracts
    53	that `a = b`) and then checks whether this is consistent. When `a`
    54	and `b` are distinct constructors (like `Nat.zero` and `Nat.succ _`),
    55	the equality is impossible, and the goal closes automatically. This
    56	pattern works for any type with distinct constructors — `Nat`, `Bool`,
    57	`Fin`, etc. — not just `Fin`.
    58	
    59	You could also prove this using `Fin.ext_iff`: `intro h; rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h; omega`.
    60	This reduces the `Fin` equality to `0 = 1` at the `ℕ` level, then
    61	`omega` sees the contradiction. Both approaches work — here's when
    62	to choose each:
    63	
    64	- **`intro h; cases h`** — use when both sides are **concrete literals**
    65	  (like `0` and `1`). The constructor discrimination is automatic.
    66	- **`intro h; rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h; omega`** — use when either side
    67	  involves a **variable** or **arithmetic expression** (like `i` or
    68	  `i + 1`). The `cases` approach won't fire on variable expressions,
    69	  but reducing to values and using `omega` always works.
    70	
    71	This is the discrimination step you'll need in the boss.
    72	
    73	**Looking ahead**: The fact that Lean can *computationally* determine
    74	whether two `Fin` elements are equal is called **decidable equality**.
    75	The `intro h; cases h` pattern works because Lean's kernel can evaluate
    76	whether the underlying constructors match. This property — `DecidableEq
    77	(Fin n)` — is what will make `Finset (Fin n)` possible in Phase 2: a
    78	finite set needs to check for duplicates, and decidable equality is what
    79	makes that check computable.
    80	"
    81	
    82	DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases tauto

=== L22_ExistentialWitness.lean ===
     1	import Game.Metadata
     2	
     3	World "MeetFin"
     4	Level 22
     5	
     6	Title "Existential Witnesses"
     7	
     8	Introduction "
     9	# Providing a Fin Element as a Witness
    10	
    11	In Level 1, you constructed a `Fin` element with `exact ⟨k, by omega⟩`.
    12	But what if the goal asks you to prove that *some* element exists
    13	with a property? Then you need `use` to provide the witness.
    14	
    15	When the goal is `∃ x : Fin n, P x`, the tactic `use ⟨k, by omega⟩`
    16	provides a specific `Fin n` element as the witness, then asks you
    17	to prove `P` holds for that element.
    18	
    19	This combines two skills you already know:
    20	- `use` from the NNG4 prerequisites (providing existential witnesses)
    21	- `⟨k, by omega⟩` from Level 1 (constructing `Fin` elements)
    22	
    23	**Your task**: Find a `Fin 5` element whose value is `3`.
    24	"
    25	
    26	/-- There exists an element of `Fin 5` with value `3`. -/
    27	Statement : ∃ x : Fin 5, x.val = 3 := by
    28	  Hint "Provide the witness with `use ⟨3, by omega⟩`. This constructs
    29	  the element `3 : Fin 5` and provides it as the existential witness.
    30	  The remaining goal `3 = 3` closes automatically."
    31	  Hint (hidden := true) "`use ⟨3, by omega⟩` provides the witness and
    32	  Lean closes `3 = 3` by reflexivity."
    33	  use ⟨3, by omega⟩
    34	
    35	Conclusion "
    36	The pattern `use ⟨k, by omega⟩` provides a `Fin` element as an
    37	existential witness. This is simply the combination of two moves:
    38	
    39	| Move | From |
    40	|---|---|
    41	| `use expr` — provide a witness | NNG4 prerequisites |
    42	| `⟨k, by omega⟩` — construct a Fin element | Level 1 |
    43	
    44	You'll use this combination in the boss.
    45	"
    46	
    47	DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases tauto

=== L23_Boss.lean ===
     1	import Game.Metadata
     2	
     3	World "MeetFin"
     4	Level 23
     5	
     6	Title "Boss: Fin Fundamentals"
     7	
     8	Introduction "
     9	# Boss: Putting It All Together
    10	
    11	Time to combine everything you've learned in a two-part challenge.
    12	
    13	**Part 1** — *Transitivity through extensionality*: If `x` and `y`
    14	have the same value, and `y = z` as Fin elements, then `x = z`. This
    15	combines `ext` on the goal (Level 15) with `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h`
    16	on hypotheses (Level 7).
    17	
    18	**Part 2** — *Non-triviality*: Fin 3 has more than one element.
    19	This requires exhaustive case analysis (Levels 16–18), existential
    20	witnesses (Level 22), inequality proving (Level 21), and the Fin
    21	contradiction pattern (Level 13) for the impossible branch.
    22	
    23	The boss tests whether you can assemble these moves into a
    24	coherent proof. No single level taught all of this at once.
    25	"
    26	
    27	/-- Fin equality is transitive through values, and Fin 3 is non-trivial. -/
    28	Statement : (∀ x y z : Fin 3, x.val = y.val → y = z → x = z) ∧
    29	            (∀ x : Fin 3, ∃ y : Fin 3, x ≠ y) := by
    30	  Hint "The goal is a conjunction. Use `constructor` to split it
    31	  into two sub-goals."
    32	  constructor
    33	  -- Part 1: transitivity through extensionality
    34	  · Hint "Start with `intro x y z h1 h2`."
    35	    intro x y z h1 h2
    36	    Hint "You need to show `x = z`. Use `ext` to convert this to
    37	    a value equality `x.val = z.val`."
    38	    ext
    39	    Hint "Now you need `x.val = z.val`. Use `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h2`
    40	    to extract `y.val = z.val` from `{h2}`."
    41	    rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h2
    42	    Hint "Now `{h1}` says `x.val = y.val` and `{h2}` says
    43	    `y.val = z.val`. `omega` can chain these."
    44	    omega
    45	  -- Part 2: non-triviality via exhaustive case analysis
    46	  · Hint "Perform exhaustive case analysis: destructure `x` with
    47	    `cases`, then split on the value. For each valid case (0, 1, 2),
    48	    choose a different `Fin 3` element as witness and prove inequality
    49	    with `intro h; cases h`. The impossible case (value >= 3) closes
    50	    with `absurd`."
    51	    intro x
    52	    Hint (hidden := true) "Destructure `x`: `cases x with | mk v hv =>`"
    53	    cases x with
    54	    | mk v hv =>
    55	      Hint (hidden := true) "Case-split on `v`:
    56	      `cases v with | zero => ... | succ n => ...`"
    57	      cases v with
    58	      | zero =>
    59	        Hint (hidden := true) "The element is `0`. Provide `1` as witness:
    60	        `use ⟨1, by omega⟩`, then prove `0 ≠ 1` with `intro h; cases h`."
    61	        use ⟨1, by omega⟩
    62	        intro h
    63	        cases h
    64	      | succ n =>
    65	        Hint (hidden := true) "The element has value `n + 1`. Split on `n`."
    66	        cases n with
    67	        | zero =>
    68	          Hint (hidden := true) "The element is `1`. Use `⟨0, by omega⟩`
    69	          as witness, then `intro h; cases h`."
    70	          use ⟨0, by omega⟩
    71	          intro h
    72	          cases h
    73	        | succ m =>
    74	          Hint (hidden := true) "The element has value `m + 2`. Split on `m`."
    75	          cases m with
    76	          | zero =>
    77	            Hint (hidden := true) "The element is `2`. Use `⟨0, by omega⟩`
    78	            as witness, then `intro h; cases h`."
    79	            use ⟨0, by omega⟩
    80	            intro h
    81	            cases h
    82	          | succ k =>
    83	            Hint (hidden := true) "Here `v = k + 3` but `hv` says `v < 3`.
    84	            This is impossible: `exact absurd hv (by omega)`."
    85	            exact absurd hv (by omega)
    86	
    87	Conclusion "
    88	Congratulations — you've completed **Meet Fin**!
    89	
    90	You now have these core moves for working with `Fin n`:
    91	
    92	| Move | Tool | Learned in |
    93	|---|---|---|
    94	| Construct a `Fin n` element | `⟨k, by omega⟩` | Level 1 |
    95	| Extract the number / coercion | `.val`, `.isLt`, `↑i` | Level 2 |
    96	| Upper bound proof | `.isLt` + `omega` | Level 3 |
    97	| Combine bounds from multiple elements | multiple `.isLt` + `omega` | Level 4 |
    98	| Prove `Fin` elements equal | `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` or `ext` | Levels 5, 15 |
    99	| Rewrite hypotheses | `rw [...] at h` | Levels 7–8 |
   100	| Derive Fin.eta | `rw [Fin.ext_iff]; rfl` | Level 10 |
   101	| Proof irrelevance | `Fin.ext_iff` + `omega` | Level 11 |
   102	| General `Fin (n+1)` reasoning | `.isLt` + `omega` | Level 12 |
   103	| Fin contradiction pattern | `.isLt` + `omega` on empty/impossible | Level 13 |
   104	| Handle `Fin 1` (singleton) | `ext` + `omega` | Level 15 |
   105	| Destructure and case-split | `cases` (nested) + `absurd` | Levels 16–18 |
   106	| Modular arithmetic | construction + `rfl` | Level 19 |
   107	| Use Fin as a concrete index | case analysis on indexed data | Level 20 |
   108	| Prove `Fin` inequality | `intro h; cases h` | Level 21 |
   109	| Existential witness | `use ⟨k, by omega⟩` | Level 22 |
   110	
   111	**Where this is going**: `Fin n` is the *canonical* finite type in
   112	Lean — the standard model that everything connects back to. Every
   113	finite type with `n` elements is equivalent to `Fin n`, making it
   114	the universal representative. A function `Fin n → α` is essentially
   115	an `n`-tuple: an ordered list of `n` elements from `α`. You'll
   116	explore this idea in the FinTuples world.
   117	
   118	Beyond tuples, you'll meet `Finset` (unordered finite collections),
   119	`Fintype` (types with finitely many elements), and big operators
   120	like `∑` and `∏` over finite index sets. All of these are built on
   121	the foundation you've just learned.
   122	
   123	(As you saw in Level 19, `Fin n` also carries modular arithmetic —
   124	addition and multiplication wrap around mod `n`, making `Fin n` a
   125	model of `ℤ/nℤ`. This course focuses on `Fin` as a finite index
   126	type, not as a number system.)
   127	
   128	In the next world, you'll learn to **navigate** within `Fin n` —
   129	moving forward with `Fin.succ`, embedding with `Fin.castSucc`, and
   130	reaching the boundary with `Fin.last`.
   131	"
   132	
   133	DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases tauto

1→=== L01_TheLastElement.lean ===
     2→     1	import Game.Metadata
     3→     2	
     4→     3	World "FinNavigation"
     5→     4	Level 1
     6→     5	
     7→     6	Title "The Last Element"
     8→     7	
     9→     8	Introduction "
    10→     9	# Fin.last: The Boundary Element
    11→    10	
    12→    11	In the previous world, you learned that `Fin n` contains the numbers
    13→    12	$0, 1, \\ldots, n-1$. But how do you name the *largest* element?
    14→    13	
    15→    14	For `Fin (n+1)`, the largest element has value `n`. Lean gives it a
    16→    15	name: **`Fin.last n`**.
    17→    16	
    18→    17	`Fin.last n` lives in `Fin (n+1)` — not `Fin n` — because `n` is
    19→    18	less than `n+1` but not less than `n`.
    20→    19	
    21→    20	The theorem **`Fin.val_last`** extracts its value:
    22→    21	$$\\texttt{(Fin.last } n\\texttt{).val} = n$$
    23→    22	
    24→    23	**Your task**: Show that `Fin.last 4` has value `4`.
    25→    24	"
    26→    25	
    27→    26	/-- `Fin.last 4` has value 4. -/
    28→    27	Statement : (Fin.last 4).val = 4 := by
    29→    28	  Hint "Use `exact Fin.val_last 4` to close this goal.
    30→    29	  (Since the definition is transparent, `rfl` also works here.
    31→    30	  But `Fin.val_last` is the general tool you'll need when `n`
    32→    31	  is a variable.)"
    33→    32	  exact Fin.val_last 4
    34→    33	
    35→    34	Conclusion "
    36→    35	`Fin.val_last n` tells you that `(Fin.last n).val = n`.
    37→    36	
    38→    37	This is the analog of saying: the largest element of
    39→    38	$\\{0, 1, \\ldots, n\\}$ is $n$. In Lean, you need `Fin.val_last`
    40→    39	to access this fact because `Fin.last` is an opaque term —
    41→    40	the type system doesn't automatically compute its value.
    42→    41	
    43→    42	In the next level, you'll use `Fin.val_last` as a **rewrite** tool
    44→    43	inside a larger proof.
    45→    44	"
    46→    45	
    47→    46	/-- `Fin.last n` is the largest element of `Fin (n + 1)`.
    48→    47	Its underlying value is `n`.
    49→    48	
    50→    49	## Construction
    51→    50	`Fin.last n : Fin (n + 1)`
    52→    51	
    53→    52	## Example
    54→    53	`Fin.last 4 : Fin 5` — the element with value 4.
    55→    54	
    56→    55	## Value
    57→    56	`(Fin.last n).val = n` (see `Fin.val_last`).
    58→    57	-/
    59→    58	DefinitionDoc Fin.last as "Fin.last"
    60→    59	
    61→    60	/-- `Fin.val_last n` states that `(Fin.last n).val = n`.
    62→    61	
    63→    62	## Syntax
    64→    63	```
    65→    64	rw [Fin.val_last]    -- rewrites (Fin.last n).val to n
    66→    65	exact Fin.val_last n -- closes a goal (Fin.last n).val = n
    67→    66	```
    68→    67	
    69→    68	## When to use it
    70→    69	When you need to compute or simplify the value of `Fin.last`.
    71→    70	
    72→    71	## Example
    73→    72	```
    74→    73	-- Goal: (Fin.last 4).val = 4
    75→    74	exact Fin.val_last 4
    76→    75	```
    77→    76	-/
    78→    77	TheoremDoc Fin.val_last as "Fin.val_last" in "Fin"
    79→    78	
    80→    79	NewDefinition Fin.last
    81→    80	NewTheorem Fin.val_last
    82→    81

    86→=== L02_LastIsMaximum.lean ===
    87→     1	import Game.Metadata
    88→     2	
    89→     3	World "FinNavigation"
    90→     4	Level 2
    91→     5	
    92→     6	Title "Last is the Maximum"
    93→     7	
    94→     8	Introduction "
    95→     9	# Using Fin.val_last as a Rewrite
    96→    10	
    97→    11	`Fin.val_last` isn't just for computing concrete values — it's a
    98→    12	**rewrite** tool. When you have `(Fin.last n).val` somewhere in
    99→    13	your goal, `rw [Fin.val_last]` replaces it with `n`, often
   100→    14	unlocking `omega` to finish the job.
   101→    15	
   102→    16	**Your task**: Prove that every element of `Fin 5` has value at
   103→    17	most `(Fin.last 4).val`.
   104→    18	
   105→    19	Strategy:
   106→    20	1. `intro i` to name the element
   107→    21	2. `rw [Fin.val_last]` to simplify the right side to `4`
   108→    22	3. `omega` to close `i.val ≤ 4` (since `i.val < 5`)
   109→    23	"
   110→    24	
   111→    25	/-- Every element of `Fin 5` has value at most `(Fin.last 4).val`. -/
   112→    26	Statement : ∀ i : Fin 5, i.val ≤ (Fin.last 4).val := by
   113→    27	  Hint "Start with `intro i` to name the element."
   114→    28	  intro i
   115→    29	  Hint "Now `rw [Fin.val_last]` converts `(Fin.last 4).val` to `4`."
   116→    30	  rw [Fin.val_last]
   117→    31	  Hint "The goal is `i.val ≤ 4`. Since `i : Fin 5`, you know
   118→    32	  `i.val < 5`, so `i.val ≤ 4`. `omega` handles this."
   119→    33	  omega
   120→    34	
   121→    35	Conclusion "
   122→    36	The **rewrite-then-omega** pattern will be your main tool in this
   123→    37	world:
   124→    38	
   125→    39	1. Use a val lemma (`Fin.val_last`, or the ones you'll learn next)
   126→    40	   to simplify `Fin` expressions to plain natural numbers
   127→    41	2. Use `omega` to close the resulting arithmetic goal
   128→    42	
   129→    43	Why is `Fin.last` the maximum? Because every `i : Fin (n+1)`
   130→    44	satisfies `i.val < n+1`, which means `i.val ≤ n`. And
   131→    45	`(Fin.last n).val = n`. So `i.val ≤ (Fin.last n).val`.
   132→    46	
   133→    47	In plain math: $\\forall\\, i \\in \\{0, \\ldots, n\\},\\; i \\leq n$.
   134→    48	"
   135→    49	
   136→    50	DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num
   137→    51	DisabledTheorem Fin.castSucc_lt_succ Fin.succ_pos Fin.le_last
