   139→=== L03_EmbeddingCastSucc.lean ===
   140→     1	import Game.Metadata
   141→     2	
   142→     3	World "FinNavigation"
   143→     4	Level 3
   144→     5	
   145→     6	Title "Embedding with castSucc"
   146→     7	
   147→     8	Introduction "
   148→     9	# Fin.castSucc: Same Value, Bigger Type
   149→    10	
   150→    11	What if you have an element of `Fin n` and need it in `Fin (n+1)`?
   151→    12	
   152→    13	**`Fin.castSucc`** does exactly this: it takes `i : Fin n` and
   153→    14	returns `i.castSucc : Fin (n+1)` with the **same value**.
   154→    15	
   155→    16	For example, if `i : Fin 3` has value `2`, then
   156→    17	`i.castSucc : Fin 4` also has value `2`. The number doesn't
   157→    18	change — only the type's upper bound increases.
   158→    19	
   159→    20	The theorem **`Fin.val_castSucc`** makes this precise:
   160→    21	$$\\texttt{i.castSucc.val} = \\texttt{i.val}$$
   161→    22	
   162→    23	Why does this work? If `i.val < n`, then certainly `i.val < n+1`.
   163→    24	So the same number satisfies the stricter bound.
   164→    25	
   165→    26	**Your task**: Prove that `castSucc` preserves the value.
   166→    27	"
   167→    28	
   168→    29	/-- `castSucc` preserves the underlying value. -/
   169→    30	Statement : ∀ i : Fin 3, (i.castSucc).val = i.val := by
   170→    31	  Hint "Start with `intro i`, then use `exact Fin.val_castSucc i`."
   171→    32	  intro i
   172→    33	  exact Fin.val_castSucc i
   173→    34	
   174→    35	Conclusion "
   175→    36	`Fin.castSucc` is an **embedding**: it maps `Fin n` into
   176→    37	`Fin (n+1)` without changing any values. Think of it as
   177→    38	*widening the frame* — the elements stay in place, but the
   178→    39	type now has room for one more element at the top.
   179→    40	
   180→    41	**Why not automatic coercion?** You might wonder why Lean doesn't
   181→    42	just automatically treat `Fin n` elements as `Fin (n+1)` elements.
   182→    43	The reason: Lean's type system doesn't have a built-in coercion
   183→    44	from `Fin n` to `Fin m` for arbitrary `n <= m`. Each embedding must
   184→    45	be explicit, and `castSucc` is the standard one for the `n -> n+1`
   185→    46	step. This explicitness prevents silent type-widening bugs.
   186→    47	
   187→    48	(Since the definition is transparent, `rfl` also works here —
   188→    49	just as it did for `Fin.val_last` in Level 1. But
   189→    50	`Fin.val_castSucc` is the tool you'll need when `n` is a
   190→    51	variable, so practice using the named lemma.)
   191→    52	
   192→    53	The element that occupies that new top position is `Fin.last n`
   193→    54	(from Level 1). So `Fin (n+1)` consists of:
   194→    55	- The images of `Fin.castSucc` (values $0, \\ldots, n-1$)
   195→    56	- `Fin.last n` (value $n$)
   196→    57	
   197→    58	You'll prove this decomposition in the boss level.
   198→    59	
   199→    60	In plain math: the inclusion $\\{0, \\ldots, n-1\\} \\hookrightarrow
   200→    61	\\{0, \\ldots, n\\}$ is the identity on elements.
   201→    62	"
   202→    63	
   203→    64	/-- `Fin.castSucc` embeds `Fin n` into `Fin (n + 1)` by keeping
   204→    65	the same value but relaxing the upper bound.
   205→    66	
   206→    67	## Syntax
   207→    68	```
   208→    69	i.castSucc      -- dot notation
   209→    70	Fin.castSucc i  -- explicit
   210→    71	```
   211→    72	
   212→    73	## Type
   213→    74	`Fin.castSucc : Fin n → Fin (n + 1)`
   214→    75	
   215→    76	## Value
   216→    77	`i.castSucc.val = i.val` (see `Fin.val_castSucc`)
   217→    78	
   218→    79	## When to use it
   219→    80	When you need to treat an element of `Fin n` as an element of
   220→    81	`Fin (n + 1)` without changing its value.
   221→    82	-/
   222→    83	DefinitionDoc Fin.castSucc as "Fin.castSucc"
   223→    84	
   224→    85	/-- `Fin.val_castSucc i` states that `i.castSucc.val = i.val`.
   225→    86	
   226→    87	## Syntax
   227→    88	```
   228→    89	rw [Fin.val_castSucc]      -- rewrites i.castSucc.val to i.val
   229→    90	exact Fin.val_castSucc i   -- closes the goal directly
   230→    91	```
   231→    92	
   232→    93	## When to use it
   233→    94	When you need to simplify `castSucc` to the underlying value.
   234→    95	-/
   235→    96	TheoremDoc Fin.val_castSucc as "Fin.val_castSucc" in "Fin"
   236→    97	
   237→    98	NewDefinition Fin.castSucc
   238→    99	NewTheorem Fin.val_castSucc
   239→   100	
   240→   101	DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num
   241→   102	DisabledTheorem Fin.castSucc_lt_succ Fin.succ_pos

   243→=== L04_MovingForwardSucc.lean ===
   244→     1	import Game.Metadata
   245→     2	
   246→     3	World "FinNavigation"
   247→     4	Level 4
   248→     5	
   249→     6	Title "Moving Forward: Fin.succ"
   250→     7	
   251→     8	Introduction "
   252→     9	# Fin.succ: The Next Element
   253→    10	
   254→    11	There's a second way to go from `Fin n` to `Fin (n+1)`:
   255→    12	**`Fin.succ`**, which **increments** the value by 1.
   256→    13	
   257→    14	If `i : Fin n` has value `k`, then `i.succ : Fin (n+1)` has
   258→    15	value `k + 1`.
   259→    16	
   260→    17	Compare with `castSucc`:
   261→    18	- `i.castSucc.val = i.val` (same value)
   262→    19	- `i.succ.val = i.val + 1` (value plus one)
   263→    20	
   264→    21	Both send `Fin n → Fin (n+1)`, but they land at different
   265→    22	positions. `castSucc` keeps you in place; `succ` moves you
   266→    23	forward by one.
   267→    24	
   268→    25	The theorem **`Fin.val_succ`** states:
   269→    26	$$\\texttt{i.succ.val} = \\texttt{i.val} + 1$$
   270→    27	
   271→    28	**Your task**: Prove this value relationship.
   272→    29	"
   273→    30	
   274→    31	/-- `Fin.succ` increments the underlying value by 1. -/
   275→    32	Statement : ∀ i : Fin 3, (i.succ).val = i.val + 1 := by
   276→    33	  Hint "Start with `intro i`, then use `exact Fin.val_succ i`."
   277→    34	  intro i
   278→    35	  exact Fin.val_succ i
   279→    36	
   280→    37	Conclusion "
   281→    38	(As with `Fin.val_last` and `Fin.val_castSucc`, `rfl` also works
   282→    39	here since the definition is transparent. But `Fin.val_succ` is
   283→    40	the rewrite tool you'll need when `n` is a variable.)
   284→    41	
   285→    42	You now have the three navigation functions and their val lemmas:
   286→    43	
   287→    44	| Function | Type | Value |
   288→    45	|---|---|---|
   289→    46	| `Fin.last n` | `Fin (n+1)` | `n` |
   290→    47	| `i.castSucc` | `Fin n → Fin (n+1)` | `i.val` (unchanged) |
   291→    48	| `i.succ` | `Fin n → Fin (n+1)` | `i.val + 1` |
   292→    49	
   293→    50	The val lemmas (`val_last`, `val_castSucc`, `val_succ`) convert
   294→    51	`Fin` navigation into plain arithmetic. The strategy for proving
   295→    52	`Fin` facts is: **rewrite to values, then omega**.
   296→    53	
   297→    54	In the next level, you'll combine these val lemmas to prove a
   298→    55	relationship between `castSucc` and `succ`.
   299→    56	"
   300→    57	
   301→    58	/-- `Fin.succ` maps `Fin n` to `Fin (n + 1)` by adding 1 to the
   302→    59	underlying value.
   303→    60	
   304→    61	## Syntax
   305→    62	```
   306→    63	i.succ      -- dot notation
   307→    64	Fin.succ i  -- explicit
   308→    65	```
   309→    66	
   310→    67	## Type
   311→    68	`Fin.succ : Fin n → Fin (n + 1)`
   312→    69	
   313→    70	## Value
   314→    71	`i.succ.val = i.val + 1` (see `Fin.val_succ`)
   315→    72	
   316→    73	## Comparison with castSucc
   317→    74	- `castSucc`: same value, bigger type
   318→    75	- `succ`: value + 1, bigger type
   319→    76	-/
   320→    77	DefinitionDoc Fin.succ as "Fin.succ"
   321→    78	
   322→    79	/-- `Fin.val_succ i` states that `i.succ.val = i.val + 1`.
   323→    80	
   324→    81	## Syntax
   325→    82	```
   326→    83	rw [Fin.val_succ]      -- rewrites i.succ.val to i.val + 1
   327→    84	exact Fin.val_succ i   -- closes the goal directly
   328→    85	```
   329→    86	
   330→    87	## When to use it
   331→    88	When you need to simplify `Fin.succ` to the underlying value.
   332→    89	-/
   333→    90	TheoremDoc Fin.val_succ as "Fin.val_succ" in "Fin"
   334→    91	
   335→    92	NewDefinition Fin.succ
   336→    93	NewTheorem Fin.val_succ
   337→    94	
   338→    95	DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num
   339→    96	DisabledTheorem Fin.castSucc_lt_succ Fin.succ_pos

   341→=== L05_CastSuccBelowSucc.lean ===
   342→     1	import Game.Metadata
   343→     2	
   344→     3	World "FinNavigation"
   345→     4	Level 5
   346→     5	
   347→     6	Title "castSucc is Below succ"
   348→     7	
   349→     8	Introduction "
   350→     9	# Combining Val Lemmas
   351→    10	
   352→    11	You now know three val lemmas: `Fin.val_last`, `Fin.val_castSucc`,
   353→    12	and `Fin.val_succ`. Time to **combine** them.
   354→    13	
   355→    14	If `i : Fin n`, then `i.castSucc` and `i.succ` both live in
   356→    15	`Fin (n+1)`. Which one is larger?
   357→    16	
   358→    17	Since `castSucc` preserves the value and `succ` adds 1, clearly
   359→    18	`castSucc` lands *below* `succ`. Let's prove this.
   360→    19	
   361→    20	**Strategy**: Rewrite both sides to values using their val lemmas,
   362→    21	then let `omega` close the arithmetic.
   363→    22	
   364→    23	1. `intro i`
   365→    24	2. `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_succ]` — converts to `i.val < i.val + 1`
   366→    25	3. `omega`
   367→    26	"
   368→    27	
   369→    28	/-- `castSucc` always lands strictly below `succ`. -/
   370→    29	Statement : ∀ i : Fin 3, i.castSucc.val < i.succ.val := by
   371→    30	  Hint "Start with `intro i`."
   372→    31	  intro i
   373→    32	  Hint "Use `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_succ]` to convert both
   374→    33	  sides to plain natural numbers."
   375→    34	  rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_succ]
   376→    35	  Hint "The goal is `i.val < i.val + 1`. `omega` closes this."
   377→    36	  omega
   378→    37	
   379→    38	Conclusion "
   380→    39	The **rewrite-to-values** strategy works for comparing any two
   381→    40	`Fin` expressions:
   382→    41	1. Apply val lemmas to simplify `Fin` navigation to ℕ arithmetic
   383→    42	2. Use `omega` to close the resulting goal
   384→    43	
   385→    44	This is the standard recipe. It works because the ordering on
   386→    45	`Fin n` is defined through values: `a < b` in `Fin` means
   387→    46	`a.val < b.val` in `ℕ`.
   388→    47	
   389→    48	The fact `i.castSucc.val < i.succ.val` means these two functions
   390→    49	never agree — they always land at different positions.
   391→    50	`castSucc` fills positions $0, \\ldots, n-1$; `succ` fills
   392→    51	positions $1, \\ldots, n$. They overlap on $1, \\ldots, n-1$
   393→    52	but at different *input* values.
   394→    53	"
   395→    54	
   396→    55	DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num
   397→    56	DisabledTheorem Fin.castSucc_lt_succ Fin.succ_pos

   399→=== L06_CastSuccNeSucc.lean ===
   400→     1	import Game.Metadata
   401→     2	
   402→     3	World "FinNavigation"
   403→     4	Level 6
   404→     5	
   405→     6	Title "castSucc and succ Never Agree"
   406→     7	
   407→     8	Introduction "
   408→     9	# Different Embeddings, Different Outputs
   409→    10	
   410→    11	In Level 5, you proved that `i.castSucc.val < i.succ.val` — the
   411→    12	value of `castSucc` is strictly less than the value of `succ`.
   412→    13	The immediate consequence: **they can never be equal**.
   413→    14	
   414→    15	If `i.castSucc = i.succ`, then their values would be equal. But
   415→    16	you just showed one is strictly less than the other. Contradiction.
   416→    17	
   417→    18	The proof uses the same value-reasoning pattern you've been
   418→    19	practicing:
   419→    20	1. `intro i h` — assume `h : i.castSucc = i.succ`
   420→    21	2. `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` — convert to values
   421→    22	3. `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_succ] at h` — simplify
   422→    23	4. `omega` — `i.val = i.val + 1` is impossible
   423→    24	
   424→    25	This is a direct corollary of Level 5's strict inequality.
   425→    26	"
   426→    27	
   427→    28	/-- `castSucc` and `succ` never produce the same output. -/
   428→    29	Statement : ∀ i : Fin 3, i.castSucc ≠ i.succ := by
   429→    30	  Hint "Start with `intro i h` to assume they're equal."
   430→    31	  intro i h
   431→    32	  Hint "Convert to values: `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h`."
   432→    33	  rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h
   433→    34	  Hint "Simplify both sides: `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_succ] at h`."
   434→    35	  rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_succ] at h
   435→    36	  Hint "Now the hypothesis says `i.val = i.val + 1`. This is impossible
   436→    37	  for natural numbers. `omega` closes the goal."
   437→    38	  omega
   438→    39	
   439→    40	Conclusion "
   440→    41	This completes the picture of how `castSucc` and `succ` relate:
   441→    42	
   442→    43	| Fact | Statement | Proved in |
   443→    44	|---|---|---|
   444→    45	| Value ordering | `i.castSucc.val < i.succ.val` | Level 5 |
   445→    46	| Disjointness | `i.castSucc /= i.succ` | This level |
   446→    47	
   447→    48	The two embedding functions `castSucc` and `succ` have **disjoint
   448→    49	images at each input** — for any given `i`, the element `castSucc`
   449→    50	lands on is always different from where `succ` lands.
   450→    51	
   451→    52	This proof used a pattern that deserves a name: the **value
   452→    53	reduction strategy for hypotheses**. When you have a hypothesis
   453→    54	`h` about `Fin` elements:
   454→    55	1. `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` — reduce to values
   455→    56	2. `rw [val_lemma, ...] at h` — simplify to ℕ arithmetic
   456→    57	3. `omega` — derive a contradiction or close the goal
   457→    58	
   458→    59	In Level 5, you used the goal-level version (rewrite the *goal*
   459→    60	to values, then `omega`). Here you used the hypothesis-level
   460→    61	version (rewrite the *hypothesis* to values, then `omega`). Both
   461→    62	are instances of the same idea: **reduce Fin reasoning to ℕ
   462→    63	arithmetic, then let omega handle it**.
   463→    64	
   464→    65	Combined with the separation facts (next levels) and the
   465→    66	decompositions (later), this means `Fin (n+1)` is neatly
   466→    67	partitioned by these two embedding functions.
   467→    68	"
   468→    69	
   469→    70	DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num

   472→=== L07_CastSuccNeverLast.lean ===
   473→     1	import Game.Metadata
   474→     2	
   475→     3	World "FinNavigation"
   476→     4	Level 7
   477→     5	
   478→     6	Title "castSucc Never Reaches Last"
   479→     7	
   480→     8	Introduction "
   481→     9	# The Key Structural Fact
   482→    10	
   483→    11	Here is the most important fact about `Fin` navigation:
   484→    12	
   485→    13	> `castSucc` and `Fin.last` produce **disjoint** elements.
   486→    14	
   487→    15	No element in the image of `castSucc` can equal `Fin.last n`.
   488→    16	Why? Because `castSucc` preserves values in $\\{0, \\ldots, n-1\\}$,
   489→    17	while `Fin.last n` has value $n$.
   490→    18	
   491→    19	To prove `j.castSucc ≠ Fin.last 3`, you need to show that
   492→    20	assuming they are equal leads to a contradiction. The strategy:
   493→    21	
   494→    22	1. `intro j h` — assume `h : j.castSucc = Fin.last 3`
   495→    23	2. `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` — convert to `j.castSucc.val = (Fin.last 3).val`
   496→    24	3. `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_last] at h` — simplify to `j.val = 3`
   497→    25	4. `omega` — contradicts `j.val < 3` (since `j : Fin 3`)
   498→    26	
   499→    27	This is the **value reasoning** pattern: convert `Fin` facts to
   500→    28	value facts, then let arithmetic close the gap.

=== L01_TheLastElement.lean ===
     1	import Game.Metadata
     2	
     3	World "FinNavigation"
     4	Level 1
     5	
     6	Title "The Last Element"
     7	
     8	Introduction "
     9	# Fin.last: The Boundary Element
    10	
    11	In the previous world, you learned that `Fin n` contains the numbers
    12	$0, 1, \\ldots, n-1$. But how do you name the *largest* element?
    13	
    14	For `Fin (n+1)`, the largest element has value `n`. Lean gives it a
    15	name: **`Fin.last n`**.
    16	
    17	`Fin.last n` lives in `Fin (n+1)` — not `Fin n` — because `n` is
    18	less than `n+1` but not less than `n`.
    19	
    20	The theorem **`Fin.val_last`** extracts its value:
    21	$$\\texttt{(Fin.last } n\\texttt{).val} = n$$
    22	
    23	**Your task**: Show that `Fin.last 4` has value `4`.
    24	"
    25	
    26	/-- `Fin.last 4` has value 4. -/
    27	Statement : (Fin.last 4).val = 4 := by
    28	  Hint "Use `exact Fin.val_last 4` to close this goal.
    29	  (Since the definition is transparent, `rfl` also works here.
    30	  But `Fin.val_last` is the general tool you'll need when `n`
    31	  is a variable.)"
    32	  exact Fin.val_last 4
    33	
    34	Conclusion "
    35	`Fin.val_last n` tells you that `(Fin.last n).val = n`.
    36	
    37	This is the analog of saying: the largest element of
    38	$\\{0, 1, \\ldots, n\\}$ is $n$. In Lean, you need `Fin.val_last`
    39	to access this fact because `Fin.last` is an opaque term —
    40	the type system doesn't automatically compute its value.
    41	
    42	In the next level, you'll use `Fin.val_last` as a **rewrite** tool
    43	inside a larger proof.
    44	"
    45	
    46	/-- `Fin.last n` is the largest element of `Fin (n + 1)`.
    47	Its underlying value is `n`.
    48	
    49	## Construction
    50	`Fin.last n : Fin (n + 1)`
    51	
    52	## Example
    53	`Fin.last 4 : Fin 5` — the element with value 4.
    54	
    55	## Value
    56	`(Fin.last n).val = n` (see `Fin.val_last`).
    57	-/
    58	DefinitionDoc Fin.last as "Fin.last"
    59	
    60	/-- `Fin.val_last n` states that `(Fin.last n).val = n`.
    61	
    62	## Syntax
    63	```
    64	rw [Fin.val_last]    -- rewrites (Fin.last n).val to n
    65	exact Fin.val_last n -- closes a goal (Fin.last n).val = n
    66	```
    67	
    68	## When to use it
    69	When you need to compute or simplify the value of `Fin.last`.
    70	
    71	## Example
    72	```
    73	-- Goal: (Fin.last 4).val = 4
    74	exact Fin.val_last 4
    75	```
    76	-/
    77	TheoremDoc Fin.val_last as "Fin.val_last" in "Fin"
    78	
    79	NewDefinition Fin.last
    80	NewTheorem Fin.val_last
    81	
    82	DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num
    83	DisabledTheorem Fin.castSucc_lt_succ Fin.succ_pos

=== L02_LastIsMaximum.lean ===
     1	import Game.Metadata
     2	
     3	World "FinNavigation"
     4	Level 2
     5	
     6	Title "Last is the Maximum"
     7	
     8	Introduction "
     9	# Using Fin.val_last as a Rewrite
    10	
    11	`Fin.val_last` isn't just for computing concrete values — it's a
    12	**rewrite** tool. When you have `(Fin.last n).val` somewhere in
    13	your goal, `rw [Fin.val_last]` replaces it with `n`, often
    14	unlocking `omega` to finish the job.
    15	
    16	**Your task**: Prove that every element of `Fin 5` has value at
    17	most `(Fin.last 4).val`.
    18	
    19	Strategy:
    20	1. `intro i` to name the element
    21	2. `rw [Fin.val_last]` to simplify the right side to `4`
    22	3. `omega` to close `i.val ≤ 4` (since `i.val < 5`)
    23	"
    24	
    25	/-- Every element of `Fin 5` has value at most `(Fin.last 4).val`. -/
    26	Statement : ∀ i : Fin 5, i.val ≤ (Fin.last 4).val := by
    27	  Hint "Start with `intro i` to name the element."
    28	  intro i
    29	  Hint "Now `rw [Fin.val_last]` converts `(Fin.last 4).val` to `4`."
    30	  rw [Fin.val_last]
    31	  Hint "The goal is `i.val ≤ 4`. Since `i : Fin 5`, you know
    32	  `i.val < 5`, so `i.val ≤ 4`. `omega` handles this."
    33	  omega
    34	
    35	Conclusion "
    36	The **rewrite-then-omega** pattern will be your main tool in this
    37	world:
    38	
    39	1. Use a val lemma (`Fin.val_last`, or the ones you'll learn next)
    40	   to simplify `Fin` expressions to plain natural numbers
    41	2. Use `omega` to close the resulting arithmetic goal
    42	
    43	Why is `Fin.last` the maximum? Because every `i : Fin (n+1)`
    44	satisfies `i.val < n+1`, which means `i.val ≤ n`. And
    45	`(Fin.last n).val = n`. So `i.val ≤ (Fin.last n).val`.
    46	
    47	In plain math: $\\forall\\, i \\in \\{0, \\ldots, n\\},\\; i \\leq n$.
    48	"
    49	
    50	DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num
    51	DisabledTheorem Fin.castSucc_lt_succ Fin.succ_pos Fin.le_last

=== L03_EmbeddingCastSucc.lean ===
     1	import Game.Metadata
     2	
     3	World "FinNavigation"
     4	Level 3
     5	
     6	Title "Embedding with castSucc"
     7	
     8	Introduction "
     9	# Fin.castSucc: Same Value, Bigger Type
    10	
    11	What if you have an element of `Fin n` and need it in `Fin (n+1)`?
    12	
    13	**`Fin.castSucc`** does exactly this: it takes `i : Fin n` and
    14	returns `i.castSucc : Fin (n+1)` with the **same value**.
    15	
    16	For example, if `i : Fin 3` has value `2`, then
    17	`i.castSucc : Fin 4` also has value `2`. The number doesn't
    18	change — only the type's upper bound increases.
    19	
    20	The theorem **`Fin.val_castSucc`** makes this precise:
    21	$$\\texttt{i.castSucc.val} = \\texttt{i.val}$$
    22	
    23	Why does this work? If `i.val < n`, then certainly `i.val < n+1`.
    24	So the same number satisfies the stricter bound.
    25	
    26	**Your task**: Prove that `castSucc` preserves the value.
    27	"
    28	
    29	/-- `castSucc` preserves the underlying value. -/
    30	Statement : ∀ i : Fin 3, (i.castSucc).val = i.val := by
    31	  Hint "Start with `intro i`, then use `exact Fin.val_castSucc i`."
    32	  intro i
    33	  exact Fin.val_castSucc i
    34	
    35	Conclusion "
    36	`Fin.castSucc` is an **embedding**: it maps `Fin n` into
    37	`Fin (n+1)` without changing any values. Think of it as
    38	*widening the frame* — the elements stay in place, but the
    39	type now has room for one more element at the top.
    40	
    41	**Why not automatic coercion?** You might wonder why Lean doesn't
    42	just automatically treat `Fin n` elements as `Fin (n+1)` elements.
    43	The reason: Lean's type system doesn't have a built-in coercion
    44	from `Fin n` to `Fin m` for arbitrary `n <= m`. Each embedding must
    45	be explicit, and `castSucc` is the standard one for the `n -> n+1`
    46	step. This explicitness prevents silent type-widening bugs.
    47	
    48	(Since the definition is transparent, `rfl` also works here —
    49	just as it did for `Fin.val_last` in Level 1. But
    50	`Fin.val_castSucc` is the tool you'll need when `n` is a
    51	variable, so practice using the named lemma.)
    52	
    53	The element that occupies that new top position is `Fin.last n`
    54	(from Level 1). So `Fin (n+1)` consists of:
    55	- The images of `Fin.castSucc` (values $0, \\ldots, n-1$)
    56	- `Fin.last n` (value $n$)
    57	
    58	You'll prove this decomposition in the boss level.
    59	
    60	In plain math: the inclusion $\\{0, \\ldots, n-1\\} \\hookrightarrow
    61	\\{0, \\ldots, n\\}$ is the identity on elements.
    62	"
    63	
    64	/-- `Fin.castSucc` embeds `Fin n` into `Fin (n + 1)` by keeping
    65	the same value but relaxing the upper bound.
    66	
    67	## Syntax
    68	```
    69	i.castSucc      -- dot notation
    70	Fin.castSucc i  -- explicit
    71	```
    72	
    73	## Type
    74	`Fin.castSucc : Fin n → Fin (n + 1)`
    75	
    76	## Value
    77	`i.castSucc.val = i.val` (see `Fin.val_castSucc`)
    78	
    79	## When to use it
    80	When you need to treat an element of `Fin n` as an element of
    81	`Fin (n + 1)` without changing its value.
    82	-/
    83	DefinitionDoc Fin.castSucc as "Fin.castSucc"
    84	
    85	/-- `Fin.val_castSucc i` states that `i.castSucc.val = i.val`.
    86	
    87	## Syntax
    88	```
    89	rw [Fin.val_castSucc]      -- rewrites i.castSucc.val to i.val
    90	exact Fin.val_castSucc i   -- closes the goal directly
    91	```
    92	
    93	## When to use it
    94	When you need to simplify `castSucc` to the underlying value.
    95	-/
    96	TheoremDoc Fin.val_castSucc as "Fin.val_castSucc" in "Fin"
    97	
    98	NewDefinition Fin.castSucc
    99	NewTheorem Fin.val_castSucc
   100	
   101	DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num
   102	DisabledTheorem Fin.castSucc_lt_succ Fin.succ_pos

=== L04_MovingForwardSucc.lean ===
     1	import Game.Metadata
     2	
     3	World "FinNavigation"
     4	Level 4
     5	
     6	Title "Moving Forward: Fin.succ"
     7	
     8	Introduction "
     9	# Fin.succ: The Next Element
    10	
    11	There's a second way to go from `Fin n` to `Fin (n+1)`:
    12	**`Fin.succ`**, which **increments** the value by 1.
    13	
    14	If `i : Fin n` has value `k`, then `i.succ : Fin (n+1)` has
    15	value `k + 1`.
    16	
    17	Compare with `castSucc`:
    18	- `i.castSucc.val = i.val` (same value)
    19	- `i.succ.val = i.val + 1` (value plus one)
    20	
    21	Both send `Fin n → Fin (n+1)`, but they land at different
    22	positions. `castSucc` keeps you in place; `succ` moves you
    23	forward by one.
    24	
    25	The theorem **`Fin.val_succ`** states:
    26	$$\\texttt{i.succ.val} = \\texttt{i.val} + 1$$
    27	
    28	**Your task**: Prove this value relationship.
    29	"
    30	
    31	/-- `Fin.succ` increments the underlying value by 1. -/
    32	Statement : ∀ i : Fin 3, (i.succ).val = i.val + 1 := by
    33	  Hint "Start with `intro i`, then use `exact Fin.val_succ i`."
    34	  intro i
    35	  exact Fin.val_succ i
    36	
    37	Conclusion "
    38	(As with `Fin.val_last` and `Fin.val_castSucc`, `rfl` also works
    39	here since the definition is transparent. But `Fin.val_succ` is
    40	the rewrite tool you'll need when `n` is a variable.)
    41	
    42	You now have the three navigation functions and their val lemmas:
    43	
    44	| Function | Type | Value |
    45	|---|---|---|
    46	| `Fin.last n` | `Fin (n+1)` | `n` |
    47	| `i.castSucc` | `Fin n → Fin (n+1)` | `i.val` (unchanged) |
    48	| `i.succ` | `Fin n → Fin (n+1)` | `i.val + 1` |
    49	
    50	The val lemmas (`val_last`, `val_castSucc`, `val_succ`) convert
    51	`Fin` navigation into plain arithmetic. The strategy for proving
    52	`Fin` facts is: **rewrite to values, then omega**.
    53	
    54	In the next level, you'll combine these val lemmas to prove a
    55	relationship between `castSucc` and `succ`.
    56	"
    57	
    58	/-- `Fin.succ` maps `Fin n` to `Fin (n + 1)` by adding 1 to the
    59	underlying value.
    60	
    61	## Syntax
    62	```
    63	i.succ      -- dot notation
    64	Fin.succ i  -- explicit
    65	```
    66	
    67	## Type
    68	`Fin.succ : Fin n → Fin (n + 1)`
    69	
    70	## Value
    71	`i.succ.val = i.val + 1` (see `Fin.val_succ`)
    72	
    73	## Comparison with castSucc
    74	- `castSucc`: same value, bigger type
    75	- `succ`: value + 1, bigger type
    76	-/
    77	DefinitionDoc Fin.succ as "Fin.succ"
    78	
    79	/-- `Fin.val_succ i` states that `i.succ.val = i.val + 1`.
    80	
    81	## Syntax
    82	```
    83	rw [Fin.val_succ]      -- rewrites i.succ.val to i.val + 1
    84	exact Fin.val_succ i   -- closes the goal directly
    85	```
    86	
    87	## When to use it
    88	When you need to simplify `Fin.succ` to the underlying value.
    89	-/
    90	TheoremDoc Fin.val_succ as "Fin.val_succ" in "Fin"
    91	
    92	NewDefinition Fin.succ
    93	NewTheorem Fin.val_succ
    94	
    95	DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num
    96	DisabledTheorem Fin.castSucc_lt_succ Fin.succ_pos

=== L05_CastSuccBelowSucc.lean ===
     1	import Game.Metadata
     2	
     3	World "FinNavigation"
     4	Level 5
     5	
     6	Title "castSucc is Below succ"
     7	
     8	Introduction "
     9	# Combining Val Lemmas
    10	
    11	You now know three val lemmas: `Fin.val_last`, `Fin.val_castSucc`,
    12	and `Fin.val_succ`. Time to **combine** them.
    13	
    14	If `i : Fin n`, then `i.castSucc` and `i.succ` both live in
    15	`Fin (n+1)`. Which one is larger?
    16	
    17	Since `castSucc` preserves the value and `succ` adds 1, clearly
    18	`castSucc` lands *below* `succ`. Let's prove this.
    19	
    20	**Strategy**: Rewrite both sides to values using their val lemmas,
    21	then let `omega` close the arithmetic.
    22	
    23	1. `intro i`
    24	2. `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_succ]` — converts to `i.val < i.val + 1`
    25	3. `omega`
    26	"
    27	
    28	/-- `castSucc` always lands strictly below `succ`. -/
    29	Statement : ∀ i : Fin 3, i.castSucc.val < i.succ.val := by
    30	  Hint "Start with `intro i`."
    31	  intro i
    32	  Hint "Use `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_succ]` to convert both
    33	  sides to plain natural numbers."
    34	  rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_succ]
    35	  Hint "The goal is `i.val < i.val + 1`. `omega` closes this."
    36	  omega
    37	
    38	Conclusion "
    39	The **rewrite-to-values** strategy works for comparing any two
    40	`Fin` expressions:
    41	1. Apply val lemmas to simplify `Fin` navigation to ℕ arithmetic
    42	2. Use `omega` to close the resulting goal
    43	
    44	This is the standard recipe. It works because the ordering on
    45	`Fin n` is defined through values: `a < b` in `Fin` means
    46	`a.val < b.val` in `ℕ`.
    47	
    48	The fact `i.castSucc.val < i.succ.val` means these two functions
    49	never agree — they always land at different positions.
    50	`castSucc` fills positions $0, \\ldots, n-1$; `succ` fills
    51	positions $1, \\ldots, n$. They overlap on $1, \\ldots, n-1$
    52	but at different *input* values.
    53	"
    54	
    55	DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num
    56	DisabledTheorem Fin.castSucc_lt_succ Fin.succ_pos

=== L06_CastSuccNeSucc.lean ===
     1	import Game.Metadata
     2	
     3	World "FinNavigation"
     4	Level 6
     5	
     6	Title "castSucc and succ Never Agree"
     7	
     8	Introduction "
     9	# Different Embeddings, Different Outputs
    10	
    11	In Level 5, you proved that `i.castSucc.val < i.succ.val` — the
    12	value of `castSucc` is strictly less than the value of `succ`.
    13	The immediate consequence: **they can never be equal**.
    14	
    15	If `i.castSucc = i.succ`, then their values would be equal. But
    16	you just showed one is strictly less than the other. Contradiction.
    17	
    18	The proof uses the same value-reasoning pattern you've been
    19	practicing:
    20	1. `intro i h` — assume `h : i.castSucc = i.succ`
    21	2. `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` — convert to values
    22	3. `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_succ] at h` — simplify
    23	4. `omega` — `i.val = i.val + 1` is impossible
    24	
    25	This is a direct corollary of Level 5's strict inequality.
    26	"
    27	
    28	/-- `castSucc` and `succ` never produce the same output. -/
    29	Statement : ∀ i : Fin 3, i.castSucc ≠ i.succ := by
    30	  Hint "Start with `intro i h` to assume they're equal."
    31	  intro i h
    32	  Hint "Convert to values: `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h`."
    33	  rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h
    34	  Hint "Simplify both sides: `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_succ] at h`."
    35	  rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_succ] at h
    36	  Hint "Now the hypothesis says `i.val = i.val + 1`. This is impossible
    37	  for natural numbers. `omega` closes the goal."
    38	  omega
    39	
    40	Conclusion "
    41	This completes the picture of how `castSucc` and `succ` relate:
    42	
    43	| Fact | Statement | Proved in |
    44	|---|---|---|
    45	| Value ordering | `i.castSucc.val < i.succ.val` | Level 5 |
    46	| Disjointness | `i.castSucc /= i.succ` | This level |
    47	
    48	The two embedding functions `castSucc` and `succ` have **disjoint
    49	images at each input** — for any given `i`, the element `castSucc`
    50	lands on is always different from where `succ` lands.
    51	
    52	This proof used a pattern that deserves a name: the **value
    53	reduction strategy for hypotheses**. When you have a hypothesis
    54	`h` about `Fin` elements:
    55	1. `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` — reduce to values
    56	2. `rw [val_lemma, ...] at h` — simplify to ℕ arithmetic
    57	3. `omega` — derive a contradiction or close the goal
    58	
    59	In Level 5, you used the goal-level version (rewrite the *goal*
    60	to values, then `omega`). Here you used the hypothesis-level
    61	version (rewrite the *hypothesis* to values, then `omega`). Both
    62	are instances of the same idea: **reduce Fin reasoning to ℕ
    63	arithmetic, then let omega handle it**.
    64	
    65	Combined with the separation facts (next levels) and the
    66	decompositions (later), this means `Fin (n+1)` is neatly
    67	partitioned by these two embedding functions.
    68	"
    69	
    70	DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num

=== L07_CastSuccNeverLast.lean ===
     1	import Game.Metadata
     2	
     3	World "FinNavigation"
     4	Level 7
     5	
     6	Title "castSucc Never Reaches Last"
     7	
     8	Introduction "
     9	# The Key Structural Fact
    10	
    11	Here is the most important fact about `Fin` navigation:
    12	
    13	> `castSucc` and `Fin.last` produce **disjoint** elements.
    14	
    15	No element in the image of `castSucc` can equal `Fin.last n`.
    16	Why? Because `castSucc` preserves values in $\\{0, \\ldots, n-1\\}$,
    17	while `Fin.last n` has value $n$.
    18	
    19	To prove `j.castSucc ≠ Fin.last 3`, you need to show that
    20	assuming they are equal leads to a contradiction. The strategy:
    21	
    22	1. `intro j h` — assume `h : j.castSucc = Fin.last 3`
    23	2. `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` — convert to `j.castSucc.val = (Fin.last 3).val`
    24	3. `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_last] at h` — simplify to `j.val = 3`
    25	4. `omega` — contradicts `j.val < 3` (since `j : Fin 3`)
    26	
    27	This is the **value reasoning** pattern: convert `Fin` facts to
    28	value facts, then let arithmetic close the gap.

#### W09: Cardinality (Lecture)

**Type**: Lecture

**Promise**: By the end of this world, the learner will be able to compute cardinalities of finsets using the card_* lemma family, and prove inclusion-exclusion for two sets.

- Apply inclusion-exclusion: |s ∪ t| + |s ∩ t| = |s| + |t|
- Use card_product for product cardinality
- Prove equality by showing ≤ in both directions (antisymmetry for ℕ)

**Inventory changes**:

- L07: `card_sdiff` — cardinality of set difference. Lesson: complement counting.
- L08: `card_product` — `|s ×ˢ t| = |s| * |t|`. Lesson: product rule for counting.
- L09 (Boss): Compute `card` of a concrete finset expression involving union, intersection, and/or product, requiring multiple card_* lemmas and arithmetic. Integration of the full card toolkit.

**Dependencies**: FinsetImage (needs card_image_le from W07)

**Pset partner**: PsetCardinality (W13)

---

     2→     1	import Game.Metadata

     4→     3	World "FinsetOperations"
     5→     4	Level 1
     6→     5	
     7→     6	Title "Union Membership"
     8→     7	
     9→     8	Introduction "
    10→     9	# Union: Combining Two Finsets
    11→    10	
    12→    11	The **union** `s ∪ t` contains every element that belongs to `s`, to `t`,
    13→    12	or to both. The membership characterization is:
    14→    13	
    15→    14	$$a \\in s \\cup t \\;\\longleftrightarrow\\; a \\in s \\;\\lor\\; a \\in t$$
    16→    15	
    17→    16	In Lean, this is `Finset.mem_union`:
    18→    17	```
    19→    18	Finset.mem_union : a ∈ s ∪ t ↔ a ∈ s ∨ a ∈ t
    20→    19	```
    21→    20	
    22→    21	The proof move: `rw [Finset.mem_union]` converts a union membership goal
    23→    22	into a disjunction. Then you choose `left` or `right` and prove membership
    24→    23	in the appropriate piece.
    25→    24	
    26→    25	**Your task**: Prove that $3 \\in \\{1, 2\\} \\cup \\{3, 4\\}$.
    27→    26	
    28→    27	Since $3$ is in the right-hand set, you'll choose `right` after
    29→    28	rewriting with `mem_union`, then prove $3 \\in \\{3, 4\\}$ using the
    30→    29	insert-peel pattern from Finset Basics.
    31→    30	"
    32→    31	
    33→    32	/-- 3 is an element of {1, 2} ∪ {3, 4}. -/
    34→    33	Statement : (3 : ℕ) ∈ ({1, 2} : Finset ℕ) ∪ {3, 4} := by
    35→    34	  Hint "Use `rw [Finset.mem_union]` to convert the union membership
    36→    35	  into a disjunction: is `3` in the left set or the right set?"
    37→    36	  rw [Finset.mem_union]
    38→    37	  Hint "The goal is a disjunction: `3` is in the left set or the right
    39→    38	  set. Since $3$ is in the right set, choose `right`."
    40→    39	  right
    41→    40	  Hint "Now prove membership in the right set. Peel the outer insert with
    42→    41	  `rw [Finset.mem_insert]`."
    43→    42	  rw [Finset.mem_insert]
    44→    43	  Hint (hidden := true) "The goal is `3 = 3 ∨ 3 ∈ singleton 4`.
    45→    44	  Choose `left` and close with `rfl`."
    46→    45	  left
    47→    46	  rfl
    48→    47	
    49→    48	Conclusion "
    50→    49	The pattern for union membership:
    51→    50	1. `rw [Finset.mem_union]` — converts `a ∈ s ∪ t` to `a ∈ s ∨ a ∈ t`
    52→    51	2. `left` or `right` — choose which set the element is in
    53→    52	3. Prove membership in that set (using `mem_insert`/`mem_singleton` from Finset Basics)
    54→    53	
    55→    54	This is the same rewrite-then-choose pattern you used for `mem_insert`,
    56→    55	but now at the level of entire sets rather than individual elements.
    57→    56	
    58→    57	In plain mathematics: to show $x \\in A \\cup B$, either show $x \\in A$ or
    59→    58	$x \\in B$.
    60→    59	"
    61→    60	
    62→    61	/-- `Finset.mem_union` states that `a ∈ s ∪ t ↔ a ∈ s ∨ a ∈ t`.
    63→    62	
    64→    63	## Syntax
    65→    64	```
    66→    65	rw [Finset.mem_union]       -- in the goal
    67→    66	rw [Finset.mem_union] at h  -- in a hypothesis
    68→    67	```
    69→    68	
    70→    69	## When to use it
    71→    70	When the goal or a hypothesis involves `x ∈ s ∪ t`. After rewriting,
    72→    71	the goal becomes a disjunction that you handle with `left`/`right`.
    73→    72	
    74→    73	## Example
    75→    74	```
    76→    75	-- Goal: 3 ∈ {1, 2} ∪ {3, 4}
    77→    76	rw [Finset.mem_union]
    78→    77	-- Goal: 3 ∈ {1, 2} ∨ 3 ∈ {3, 4}
    79→    78	right
    80→    79	-- Goal: 3 ∈ {3, 4}
    81→    80	```
    82→    81	-/
    83→    82	TheoremDoc Finset.mem_union as "Finset.mem_union" in "Finset"
    84→    83	
    85→    84	/-- `Finset.union s t` (written `s ∪ t`) is the finset containing all
    86→    85	elements that belong to `s`, to `t`, or to both.
    87→    86	
    88→    87	## Notation
    89→    88	`s ∪ t` — the union of finsets `s` and `t`.
    90→    89	
    91→    90	## Key lemma
    92→    91	`Finset.mem_union : a ∈ s ∪ t ↔ a ∈ s ∨ a ∈ t`
    93→    92	
    94→    93	## Properties
    95→    94	- Union is commutative: `s ∪ t = t ∪ s`
    96→    95	- Union is associative: `(s ∪ t) ∪ u = s ∪ (t ∪ u)`
    97→    96	- Union with empty: `s ∪ ∅ = s`
    98→    97	- Union is idempotent: `s ∪ s = s`
    99→    98	-/

   110→     1	import Game.Metadata
   111→     2	
   112→     3	World "FinsetOperations"
   113→     4	Level 2
   114→     5	
   115→     6	Title "Intersection Membership"
   116→     7	
   117→     8	Introduction "
   118→     9	# Intersection: Elements in Both Sets
   119→    10	
   120→    11	The **intersection** `s ∩ t` contains only the elements that belong to
   121→    12	*both* `s` and `t`. The membership characterization is:
   122→    13	
   123→    14	$$a \\in s \\cap t \\;\\longleftrightarrow\\; a \\in s \\;\\land\\; a \\in t$$
   124→    15	
   125→    16	In Lean, this is `Finset.mem_inter`:
   126→    17	```
   127→    18	Finset.mem_inter : a ∈ s ∩ t ↔ a ∈ s ∧ a ∈ t
   128→    19	```
   129→    20	
   130→    21	The proof move: `rw [Finset.mem_inter]` converts an intersection
   131→    22	membership goal into a conjunction. Then you use `constructor` to split
   132→    23	the conjunction into two goals (membership in `s` and membership in `t`),
   133→    24	and prove each one separately.
   134→    25	
   135→    26	**Your task**: Prove that $2 \\in \\{1, 2, 3\\} \\cap \\{2, 4, 6\\}$.
   136→    27	"
   137→    28	
   138→    29	/-- 2 is in the intersection of {1, 2, 3} and {2, 4, 6}. -/
   139→    30	Statement : (2 : ℕ) ∈ ({1, 2, 3} : Finset ℕ) ∩ {2, 4, 6} := by
   140→    31	  Hint "Use `rw [Finset.mem_inter]` to convert the intersection
   141→    32	  membership into a conjunction."
   142→    33	  rw [Finset.mem_inter]
   143→    34	  Hint "The goal is a conjunction: `2` is in the left set AND `2` is in
   144→    35	  the right set. Use `constructor` to split it into two goals."
   145→    36	  constructor
   146→    37	  · Hint "Prove membership in the left set. Peel inserts:
   147→    38	    `rw [Finset.mem_insert]`, then `right`,
   148→    39	    then `rw [Finset.mem_insert]`, then `left; rfl`."
   149→    40	    rw [Finset.mem_insert]
   150→    41	    right
   151→    42	    rw [Finset.mem_insert]
   152→    43	    left
   153→    44	    rfl
   154→    45	  · Hint "Prove membership in the right set. Since $2$ is the first
   155→    46	    element, one peel suffices."
   156→    47	    Hint (hidden := true) "`rw [Finset.mem_insert]; left; rfl`."
   157→    48	    rw [Finset.mem_insert]
   158→    49	    left
   159→    50	    rfl
   160→    51	
   161→    52	Conclusion "
   162→    53	The pattern for intersection membership:
   163→    54	1. `rw [Finset.mem_inter]` — converts `a ∈ s ∩ t` to `a ∈ s ∧ a ∈ t`
   164→    55	2. `constructor` — splits the conjunction into two subgoals
   165→    56	3. Prove each membership separately
   166→    57	
   167→    58	Notice the symmetry with union:
   168→    59	- **Union** produces a disjunction (`∨`) — you choose one side
   169→    60	- **Intersection** produces a conjunction (`∧`) — you prove both sides
   170→    61	
   171→    62	In plain mathematics: to show $x \\in A \\cap B$, show $x \\in A$ *and* $x \\in B$.
   172→    63	"
   173→    64	
   174→    65	/-- `Finset.mem_inter` states that `a ∈ s ∩ t ↔ a ∈ s ∧ a ∈ t`.
   175→    66	
   176→    67	## Syntax
   177→    68	```
   178→    69	rw [Finset.mem_inter]       -- in the goal
   179→    70	rw [Finset.mem_inter] at h  -- in a hypothesis
   180→    71	```
   181→    72	
   182→    73	## When to use it
   183→    74	When the goal or a hypothesis involves `x ∈ s ∩ t`. After rewriting,
   184→    75	the goal becomes a conjunction that you handle with `constructor`.
   185→    76	
   186→    77	## Example
   187→    78	```
   188→    79	-- Goal: 2 ∈ {1, 2, 3} ∩ {2, 4, 6}
   189→    80	rw [Finset.mem_inter]
   190→    81	-- Goal: 2 ∈ {1, 2, 3} ∧ 2 ∈ {2, 4, 6}
   191→    82	constructor
   192→    83	-- Two subgoals: prove each membership
   193→    84	```
   194→    85	-/
   195→    86	TheoremDoc Finset.mem_inter as "Finset.mem_inter" in "Finset"
   196→    87	
   197→    88	/-- `Finset.inter s t` (written `s ∩ t`) is the finset containing all
   198→    89	elements that belong to both `s` and `t`.
   199→    90

content.thinking:
Now I have a comprehensive understanding of all 8 worlds. Let me also quickly scan the FinsetOperations content for the more advanced levels (especially the lattice notation, DeMorgan, distributivity levels), and the FinsetImage content.

Let me focus on writing the enrichment review now. I have enough material to work with. Let me summarize what I've found across all 8 worlds and identify enrichment opportunities.

## World Summary

### MeetFin (23 levels)
- L01-L04: Construction, extraction, bounds
- L05-L08: Fin equality (ext_iff), rewriting hypotheses


- L09-L11: Fin.eta and proof irrelevance
- L12: General Fin (n+1)
- L13-L14: Fin 0 and the explosion principle
- L15: Fin 1 as a singleton, introducing the `ext` tactic
- L16-L18: Destructuring and case analysis on Fin 2 and Fin 3
- L19: Modular arithmetic as an enrichment topic
- L20: Concrete application with functions on Fin 3
- L21: Inequality proofs using intro and cases
- L22: Existential witnesses
- L23: Boss level

### FinNav

Now moving into navigation within Fin types across 15 levels. Starting with Fin.last and establishing it as the maximum element, then exploring castSucc as an embedding operation and succ for moving forward. I'm examining how castSucc and succ relate to each other—they're distinct, and castSucc never reaches the last element. Both operations are injective, and I'm working through the decomposition of elements into either 0 or a successor, proving that succ never produces zero. The later levels focus on recognizing the last element, finding castSucc predecessors, and establishing surjectivity of castSucc on all non-last elements, culminating in a final boss challenge.

### FinTuples

Starting with functions as tuples and Vec notation, then building tuples manually...

Continuing through tuple operations like cons and tail, with lemmas about accessing and reconstructing tuples from their head and tail components. I'm also exploring snoc and init for working from the end, along with extensionality for functions and tuple composition. The injectivity of both cons and snoc operations gets established, and I'm developing strategies for proving tuple equality through flipping equations and reconstruction, finishing with another boss level.

### PsetFin

Working through retrieval problems involving witnesses, properties of castSucc, snoc reconstruction, antisymmetric equality, and pointwise formulas. I'm handling vacuous truth cases and the fact that succ never produces zero, then moving into destructuring with obtain and zero/succ retrieval...

Modular equality and general decomposition strategies for zero/succ cases, where obtain meets the decomposition framework. I'm tackling permutation problems including permutation algebra and order, a concrete pigeonhole principle, and another boss level.

### FinsetBasics

Exploring what finsets are, finding deeper elements, and the empty finset. I'm working with ranges—their boundaries, empty cases, variable ranges, and reverse direction behavior. Then bridging from Fin to Finset, proving non-membership both generally and specifically, reading membership conditions, and idempotence of insert. I'm examining nonempty finsets and how to prove they're nonempty...

### FinsetOperations

Moving into union, intersection, set difference, and filter operations on membership. Subset relations and their monotonicity properties, then equality through antisymmetry and extensionality. I'm exploring intersection idempotency, empty set identities, and general idempotence, followed by commutativity of intersection and union, union associativity, and lattice notation. Targeted simplification with simp, De Morgan's laws, proof by contradiction, complement via set difference, absorption laws, and distributivity with its dual form leading to another boss level.

### FinsetImage

Now working with image operations—starting with finding witnesses and determining membership in images. Images grow monotonically with sets, distribute over unions, and have specific behavior with singletons. Intersection properties under images, constant functions and their image collapse, then injectivity and how it preserves cardinality. I'm proving injectivity itself and understanding why it matters, especially for image intersection equality. Cardinality preservation works in reverse to establish injectivity through counting, culminating in a final boss level.

### PsetFinset

Combining all finset techniques across nine fresh problems, then a boss level involving image preservation under set difference with injectivity.

### Enrichment Opportunities

I'm noticing some gaps in mathematical depth. The FinNavigation decompositions (castSucc/last and 0/succ) are both proved separately, but their equivalence through an order-reversing bijection isn't explored. In FinsetImage, the image intersection subset result could better foreshadow the equality case under injectivity rather than treating them as disconnected results. There's also a converse pigeonhole principle worth examining in PsetFin.

The cardinality of Fin n is deferred to a later world, though FinTuples and MeetFin lay the groundwork. I should also trace the connection between Finset.univ for Fin n and Finset.range more explicitly, since they're essentially the same structure but approached differently.

Concrete examples would strengthen the material—like showing which elements collide under squaring on Fin 5, or instantiating De Morgan's laws on specific finsets rather than abstractly. The isomorphism between Bool and Fin 2 appears in MeetFin but deserves a proper proof and application. There's also a recurring "rewrite to values then omega" strategy throughout MeetFin and FinNavigation that could be formalized as a named technique.

The "unfold, extract, reassemble" pattern shows up repeatedly across FinsetOperations and PsetFinset—a consistent ext → simp only → logic structure that deserves recognition as a deliberate proof strategy. I'm noticing pedagogical gaps too: Fin.eta's name comes from η-reduction in lambda calculus but that connection is never explained, and extensionality appears across three different contexts (Fin elements, functions, finsets) where the unifying principle of "reducing to components" could be made explicit.

The lattice structure of finsets is introduced in FinsetOperations L15 more as a gotcha than as a genuine insight—the fact that finsets form a distributive lattice is mathematically rich and worth highlighting. There's also room to explore what breaks when we drop injectivity assumptions in image results, making the hypotheses feel less arbitrary. The cardinality results at the end of FinsetImage (card_image_iff showing card preservation ↔ injectivity) are exactly the counting principles that would anchor the Cardinality world, so foreshadowing that connection could strengthen the bridge between worlds. Permutation composition in PsetFin L14-L16 similarly points toward the symmetric group structure that would come later.

FinsetOperations proves all the Boolean algebra laws through its operations, and wrapping up with an explicit mention that Finset is a Boolean algebra would seed important vocabulary. On the tactic side, obtain appears heavily in later worlds but isn't introduced until PsetFin L08, and by_contra is introduced even later in FinsetOperations L18—both could benefit from earlier or more explicit treatment to prepare for their heavy use downstream.

Now I'm ranking the most impactful enrichments. The biggest gap seems to be converse and failure questions—many levels prove one direction of an implication without exploring whether the reverse holds, like why image distributes over union but only contains intersection, or whether injectivity preserves cardinality in both directions.

Another key pattern I'm noticing is the "rewrite-to-values" strategy that appears throughout MeetFin and FinNavigation but never gets named as its own concept. Making that explicit as a meta-skill would help. I should also consider adding counterexample levels that show when implications break down, and maybe one observation connecting the Boolean algebra laws in FinsetOperations to the broader lattice structure that comes later in the course.

The extensionality principle keeps showing up too—Fin.ext, funext, Finset.ext—each time reducing equality to component-wise comparison. Naming that as a unifying principle would make the pattern clearer.

Let me organize this properly by going through each enrichment lens and finding concrete, actionable suggestions for the review.

   401→#### W10: AbstractionLadder (Lecture)
   402→
   403→**Type**: Lecture
   404→
   405→**Promise**: By the end of this world, the learner will understand the three-layer abstraction: `List α` (ordered, with duplicates) -> `Multiset α` (unordered, with duplicates) -> `Finset α` (unordered, no duplicates), and why each layer exists.
   406→
   407→**Theorem families**:
   408→- `List.length`, `List.mem`, `List.append`
   409→- `List.Perm` — permutation relation
   410→- `List.Nodup`
   411→- `Multiset.card`, `Multiset.count`
   412→- `List.toFinset`, `Multiset.toFinset`
   413→- Relationship: `Finset.val` gives the underlying `Multiset`
   414→
   415→**Proof-move goals**:
   416→- Work with basic list operations
   417→- Prove two lists are permutations
   418→- Show that `Nodup` connects lists to finsets
   419→- Show what each quotient forgets (order, then duplicates)
   420→- Convert between layers
   421→
   422→**Inventory changes**:
   423→- `NewDefinition List Multiset List.Perm List.Nodup`
   424→- `NewTheorem List.length_append Multiset.card_cons List.Perm.length_eq`
   425→- `TheoremTab "List" "Multiset"`
   426→
   427→**Level sketch**:
   428→- L01: Lists: `[1, 2, 3]` has length 3, `1 ∈ [1, 2, 3]`. Lesson: basic list operations.
   429→- L02: Lists are ordered: `[1, 2, 3] ≠ [3, 1, 2]`. Lesson: order matters for equality.
   430→- L03: `List.Perm` — `[1, 2, 3] ~ [3, 1, 2]`. Lesson: permutation ignores order.
   431→- L04: `Multiset` — quotient of `List` by `Perm`. `{1, 2, 3} = {3, 1, 2}` as multisets. Lesson: order forgotten.
   432→- L05: Multisets keep duplicates: `Multiset.count 2 {1, 2, 2, 3} = 2`. Lesson: multiplicity matters.
   433→- L06: `List.Nodup` — a list with no duplicates. `[1, 2, 3].Nodup` but not `[1, 2, 2, 3].Nodup`. Lesson: the bridge to finsets.
   434→- L07: `List.toFinset` / `Multiset.toFinset` — how to get from lists/multisets to finsets. Lesson: the quotient forgets duplicates.
   435→- L08: Contrast: `[1, 2, 2, 3]` as list (length 4), as multiset (card 4), as finset (card 3). Lesson: the full picture.
   436→- L09 (Boss): A proof that requires understanding which layer to work in and converting between them. E.g., prove a fact about `List.toFinset` that requires reasoning about `Nodup` and `Perm`.
   437→
   438→**Misconceptions addressed**: List ≠ Multiset (order matters); Multiset ≠ Finset (duplicates matter); the three types have different `card`/`length`.

   446→#### W11: Fintype (Lecture)
   447→
   448→**Type**: Lecture
   449→
   450→**Promise**: By the end of this world, the learner will understand `Fintype α` as a typeclass asserting that `α` has finitely many elements, and will be able to use `Finset.univ`, `Fintype.card`, and the composition instances.
   451→
   452→**World intro note**: The introduction text must include a clear warning that not every type is finite: `ℕ` has no `Fintype` instance. Attempting `Fintype.card ℕ` is a type error. This is a core misconception — learners who have only worked with `Fin n` may assume finiteness is universal.
   453→
   454→**Theorem families**:
   455→- `Fintype.card`, `Fintype.elems`
   456→- `Finset.univ` — the finset of all elements
   457→- `Finset.mem_univ` — everything is in `univ`
   458→- `Fintype.card_fin` — `Fintype.card (Fin n) = n`
   459→- `Fintype.card_prod` — `card (α × β) = card α * card β`
   460→- `Fintype.card_sum` — `card (α ⊕ β) = card α + card β`
   461→- `Fintype.card_fun` — `card (α -> β) = card β ^ card α`
   462→- `Fintype.card_congr` — `α ≃ β -> card α = card β` (first bijective counting)
   463→
   464→**Proof-move goals**:
   465→- Use `Finset.univ` and `mem_univ` to access all elements
   466→- Compute `Fintype.card` for built-in types (`Fin n`, `Bool`, `Unit`)
   467→- Use composition instances: products, sums, subtypes
   468→- Use `Fintype.card_congr` for bijective counting (first Equiv-based proof)
   469→- Understand that `ℕ` is NOT a `Fintype` — not every type is finite
   470→
   471→**Inventory changes**:
   472→- `NewDefinition Fintype Finset.univ Fintype.card`
   473→- `NewTheorem Finset.mem_univ Fintype.card_fin Fintype.card_prod Fintype.card_sum Fintype.card_fun Fintype.card_congr`
   474→- `TheoremTab "Fintype"`
   475→
   476→**Level sketch**:
   477→- L01: `Fintype (Fin n)` — `Fin n` is finite. `Fintype.card (Fin n) = n`. Lesson: the card theorem.
   478→- L02: `Finset.univ` — the set of all elements. `∀ x : Fin n, x ∈ Finset.univ`. Lesson: `mem_univ`.
   479→- L03: `Fintype Bool` — `Fintype.card Bool = 2`. Lesson: other finite types.
   480→- L04: Products are finite — `card (Fin 2 × Fin 3) = 6`. Lesson: `card_prod`.
   481→- L05: Sums are finite — `card (Fin 2 ⊕ Fin 3) = 5`. Lesson: `card_sum`.
   482→- L06: Function types — `card (Fin 2 -> Fin 3) = 9`. Lesson: `card_fun` = m^n.
   483→- L07: ℕ is NOT finite — `ℕ` has no `Fintype` instance. Show that attempting `Fintype.card ℕ` fails. Lesson: finiteness is a property, not a given. Warning: not every type you know is finite.
   484→- L08: `Fintype.card_congr` — if `α ≃ β` then `card α = card β`. Lesson: bijective counting. This is the first Equiv-based argument.
   485→- L09 (Boss): Compute the cardinality of a composite type (e.g., `Fin 2 × Fin 3 -> Bool`) using multiple card_* lemmas.
   486→
   487→**Misconceptions addressed**: `ℕ` has no `Fintype` instance — not every type is finite.

   500→

   517→- L04: Counting subsets: `card (Fin n -> Bool) = 2^n` — functions to `Bool` are subsets. Motivation for powerset counting.
   518→- L05: Introduce `Nat.descFactorial` — the falling factorial `n * (n-1) * ... * (n-k+1)`. Count injections `Fin k ↪ Fin n` for small k, n. Connection to `Nat.descFactorial`.

   551→### Phase 4: Big Operators (Worlds 14-18)
   552→

   554→
   555→#### W14: BigOpIntro (Lecture)
   556→
   557→**Type**: Lecture
   558→
   559→**Promise**: By the end of this world, the learner will understand `∑ x ∈ s, f x` and `∏ x ∈ s, f x`, know the constructor lemmas for empty/singleton/insert, and be able to unfold sums over small concrete finsets.
   560→
   561→**Theorem families**:
   562→- `Finset.sum_empty`, `Finset.prod_empty`
   563→- `Finset.sum_singleton`, `Finset.prod_singleton`
   564→- `Finset.sum_insert`, `Finset.prod_insert`
   565→- `Finset.sum_congr`, `Finset.prod_congr`
   566→
   567→**Proof-move goals**:
   568→- Read and write `∑ x ∈ s, f x` notation
   569→- Unfold a sum/product over a small finset to individual terms
   570→- Use `sum_insert` to peel off one element
   571→- Use `sum_congr` to rewrite the summand
   572→

   574→- `NewDefinition Finset.sum Finset.prod`
   575→- `NewTheorem Finset.sum_empty Finset.sum_singleton Finset.sum_insert Finset.sum_congr Finset.prod_empty Finset.prod_singleton Finset.prod_insert Finset.prod_congr`
   576→- `TheoremTab "BigOp"`
   577→- Disable: `norm_num` on early levels (force manual sum unfolding)
   578→
   579→**Level sketch**:
   580→- L01: Notation — what does `∑ x ∈ s, f x` mean? Read the goal state. Lesson: big-operator notation.
   581→- L02: `sum_empty` — `∑ x ∈ ∅, f x = 0`. Lesson: base case is 0 (not undefined!). Warning: empty sum = additive identity.
   582→- L03: `sum_singleton` — `∑ x ∈ {a}, f x = f a`. Lesson: sum over singleton.
   583→- L04: `sum_insert` — `a ∉ s -> ∑ x ∈ insert a s, f x = f a + ∑ x ∈ s, f x`. Lesson: peeling off one element.
   584→- L05: Unfold `∑ x ∈ {1, 2, 3}, f x` completely using repeated `sum_insert`. Lesson: concrete computation.
   585→- L06: `prod_empty`, `prod_singleton`, `prod_insert` — same story for products. Lesson: multiplicative analogues. Warning: empty product = 1.
   586→- L07: `sum_congr` — rewrite the summand pointwise. Lesson: rewriting under a binder.
   587→- L08: Connection to the abstraction ladder — `Finset.sum` is defined via `Multiset.sum`, which is defined via `List.sum`. Show that `∑ x ∈ s, f x` ultimately reduces through the Multiset layer. Lesson: retrieval of the List → Multiset → Finset pipeline from W10 in a new context. This is why the abstraction ladder matters — big operators sit on top of it.
   588→- L09 (Boss): Compute a sum over a specific finset by unfolding with `sum_insert`, rewriting with `sum_congr`, and simplifying. Integration of all constructor lemmas.
   589→
   590→**Misconceptions addressed**: Sum over empty set is 0, not undefined; product over empty set is 1.
   591→
   592→**Dependencies**: FinsetOperations (needs membership, insert)
   593→

#### W10: AbstractionLadder (Lecture)

**Type**: Lecture

**Promise**: By the end of this world, the learner will understand the three-layer abstraction: `List α` (ordered, with duplicates) -> `Multiset α` (unordered, with duplicates) -> `Finset α` (unordered, no duplicates), and why each layer exists.

**Theorem families**:
- `List.length`, `List.mem`, `List.append`
- `List.Perm` — permutation relation
- `List.Nodup`
- `Multiset.card`, `Multiset.count`
- `List.toFinset`, `Multiset.toFinset`
- Relationship: `Finset.val` gives the underlying `Multiset`

**Proof-move goals**:
- Work with basic list operations
- Prove two lists are permutations
- Show that `Nodup` connects lists to finsets
- Show what each quotient forgets (order, then duplicates)
- Convert between layers

**Inventory changes**:
- `NewDefinition List Multiset List.Perm List.Nodup`
- `NewTheorem List.length_append Multiset.card_cons List.Perm.length_eq`
- `TheoremTab "List" "Multiset"`

**Level sketch**:
- L01: Lists: `[1, 2, 3]` has length 3, `1 ∈ [1, 2, 3]`. Lesson: basic list operations.
- L02: Lists are ordered: `[1, 2, 3] ≠ [3, 1, 2]`. Lesson: order matters for equality.
- L03: `List.Perm` — `[1, 2, 3] ~ [3, 1, 2]`. Lesson: permutation ignores order.
- L04: `Multiset` — quotient of `List` by `Perm`. `{1, 2, 3} = {3, 1, 2}` as multisets. Lesson: order forgotten.
- L05: Multisets keep duplicates: `Multiset.count 2 {1, 2, 2, 3} = 2`. Lesson: multiplicity matters.
- L06: `List.Nodup` — a list with no duplicates. `[1, 2, 3].Nodup` but not `[1, 2, 2, 3].Nodup`. Lesson: the bridge to finsets.
- L07: `List.toFinset` / `Multiset.toFinset` — how to get from lists/multisets to finsets. Lesson: the quotient forgets duplicates.
- L08: Contrast: `[1, 2, 2, 3]` as list (length 4), as multiset (card 4), as finset (card 3). Lesson: the full picture.
- L09 (Boss): A proof that requires understanding which layer to work in and converting between them. E.g., prove a fact about `List.toFinset` that requires reasoning about `Nodup` and `Perm`.

**Misconceptions addressed**: List ≠ Multiset (order matters); Multiset ≠ Finset (duplicates matter); the three types have different `card`/`length`.

**Dependencies**: Cardinality

**Pset partner**: PsetCardinality (W13)

#### W11: Fintype (Lecture)

**Type**: Lecture

**Promise**: By the end of this world, the learner will understand `Fintype α` as a typeclass asserting that `α` has finitely many elements, and will be able to use `Finset.univ`, `Fintype.card`, and the composition instances.

**World intro note**: The introduction text must include a clear warning that not every type is finite: `ℕ` has no `Fintype` instance. Attempting `Fintype.card ℕ` is a type error. This is a core misconception — learners who have only worked with `Fin n` may assume finiteness is universal.

**Theorem families**:
- `Fintype.card`, `Fintype.elems`
- `Finset.univ` — the finset of all elements
- `Finset.mem_univ` — everything is in `univ`
- `Fintype.card_fin` — `Fintype.card (Fin n) = n`
- `Fintype.card_prod` — `card (α × β) = card α * card β`
- `Fintype.card_sum` — `card (α ⊕ β) = card α + card β`
- `Fintype.card_fun` — `card (α -> β) = card β ^ card α`
- `Fintype.card_congr` — `α ≃ β -> card α = card β` (first bijective counting)

**Proof-move goals**:
- Use `Finset.univ` and `mem_univ` to access all elements
- Compute `Fintype.card` for built-in types (`Fin n`, `Bool`, `Unit`)
- Use composition instances: products, sums, subtypes
- Use `Fintype.card_congr` for bijective counting (first Equiv-based proof)
- Understand that `ℕ` is NOT a `Fintype` — not every type is finite

**Inventory changes**:

- `NewTheorem Finset.mem_univ Fintype.card_fin Fintype.card_prod Fintype.card_sum Fintype.card_fun Fintype.card_congr`
- `TheoremTab "Fintype"`

**Level sketch**:
- L01: `Fintype (Fin n)` — `Fin n` is finite. `Fintype.card (Fin n) = n`. Lesson: the card theorem.
- L02: `Finset.univ` — the set of all elements. `∀ x : Fin n, x ∈ Finset.univ`. Lesson: `mem_univ`.
- L03: `Fintype Bool` — `Fintype.card Bool = 2`. Lesson: other finite types.
- L04: Products are finite — `card (Fin 2 × Fin 3) = 6`. Lesson: `card_prod`.
- L05: Sums are finite — `card (Fin 2 ⊕ Fin 3) = 5`. Lesson: `card_sum`.
- L06: Function types — `card (Fin 2 -> Fin 3) = 9`. Lesson: `card_fun` = m^n.
- L07: ℕ is NOT finite — `ℕ` has no `Fintype` instance. Show that attempting `Fintype.card ℕ` fails. Lesson: finiteness is a property, not a given. Warning: not every type you know is finite.
- L08: `Fintype.card_congr` — if `α ≃ β` then `card α = card β`. Lesson: bijective counting. This is the first Equiv-based argument.

#### W12: CountingTypes (Example)

**Type**: Example / Case-study

**Promise**: By the end of this world, the learner will have computed cardinalities of several concrete finite types and connected abstract card_* theorems to concrete numbers.

- L02: `card (Fin 3 -> Fin 2) = 8 = 2^3`. Functions as assignments. Concretization.

- L04: Counting subsets: `card (Fin n -> Bool) = 2^n` — functions to `Bool` are subsets. Motivation for powerset counting.
- L05: Introduce `Nat.descFactorial` — the falling factorial `n * (n-1) * ... * (n-k+1)`. Count injections `Fin k ↪ Fin n` for small k, n. Connection to `Nat.descFactorial`.
- L06: Inclusion-exclusion practice — compute `card (A ∪ B)` for concrete finsets `A` and `B` where `A ∩ B` is nonempty. Apply `card_union_add_card_inter` from W09 in a Fintype/counting context. Lesson: scaffolded retrieval of inclusion-exclusion on fresh surface.

### Phase 4: Big Operators (Worlds 14-18)

#### W14: BigOpIntro (Lecture)

**Type**: Lecture

**Promise**: By the end of this world, the learner will understand `∑ x ∈ s, f x` and `∏ x ∈ s, f x`, know the constructor lemmas for empty/singleton/insert, and be able to unfold sums over small concrete finsets.

- Read and write `∑ x ∈ s, f x` notation
- Unfold a sum/product over a small finset to individual terms
- Use `sum_insert` to peel off one element

**Inventory changes**:
- `NewDefinition Finset.sum Finset.prod`
- `NewTheorem Finset.sum_empty Finset.sum_singleton Finset.sum_insert Finset.sum_congr Finset.prod_empty Finset.prod_singleton Finset.prod_insert Finset.prod_congr`

- L04: `sum_insert` — `a ∉ s -> ∑ x ∈ insert a s, f x = f a + ∑ x ∈ s, f x`. Lesson: peeling off one element.
- L05: Unfold `∑ x ∈ {1, 2, 3}, f x` completely using repeated `sum_insert`. Lesson: concrete computation.
- L06: `prod_empty`, `prod_singleton`, `prod_insert` — same story for products. Lesson: multiplicative analogues. Warning: empty product = 1.
- L07: `sum_congr` — rewrite the summand pointwise. Lesson: rewriting under a binder.
- L08: Connection to the abstraction ladder — `Finset.sum` is defined via `Multiset.sum`, which is defined via `List.sum`. Show that `∑ x ∈ s, f x` ultimately reduces through the Multiset layer. Lesson: retrieval of the List → Multiset → Finset pipeline from W10 in a new context. This is why the abstraction ladder matters — big operators sit on top of it.
- L09 (Boss): Compute a sum over a specific finset by unfolding with `sum_insert`, rewriting with `sum_congr`, and simplifying. Integration of all constructor lemmas.

**Misconceptions addressed**: Sum over empty set is 0, not undefined; product over empty set is 1.

**Dependencies**: FinsetOperations (needs membership, insert)

# Enrichment Review: finite_math — MeetFin & FinNavigation

**Note**: Only MeetFin (21 levels) and FinNavigation (10 levels) have been authored. The remaining 27 worlds in the list have no level files and cannot be reviewed.

---

## Ranked Suggestions

### 1. FinNavigation: Generalize the decomposition beyond concrete types

**What**: State the last-or-castSucc decomposition for general `Fin (n+1)`, not just `Fin 3`.

**Why**: The entire FinNavigation world uses concrete types (`Fin 3`, `Fin 4`, `Fin 5`) — including the boss. MeetFin has L11 "Beyond Concrete Types" that deliberately generalizes to `Fin (n+1)` with variable `n`, proving that the techniques transfer. FinNavigation never makes this move. The decomposition `∀ i : Fin (n+1), i = Fin.last n ∨ ∃ j : Fin n, i = j.castSucc` is the structural heart of the world — and the general statement is what actually gets used in later worlds (FinTuples, Fintype). Leaving it at `Fin 3` undersells the result. The plan itself calls for "a statement about `Fin (n+1)`" in the boss.

**Where**: New level before the boss (L10), or replace the boss Part 1 with the general statement. The general proof uses `omega` on `i.val` with hypothesis `i.val < n + 1`, branching on `i.val < n` vs `i.val = n` — same pattern as the concrete version.

**Effort**: Medium (modify boss or add a level)

**Recommend**: Yes

---

### 2. MeetFin: Teach `obtain` as a lighter destructuring syntax

**What**: Introduce `obtain ⟨v, hlt⟩ := x` as an alternative to `cases x with | mk v hlt =>`.

**Why**: L15 introduces destructuring with the verbose pattern `cases x with | mk v hlt =>`, and then L16-L21 repeat this pattern in every case analysis. The `obtain` tactic does the same thing with cleaner syntax and is the idiom experienced Lean users actually reach for. Teaching both gives the learner a choice and builds a real-Lean skill that transfers to every subtype, sigma type, and existential they'll encounter. The novelty budget permits this: destructuring is the only new concept in L15, so adding an `obtain`-based alternative in the conclusion (with a follow-up level using `obtain`) fits within one-new-thing-per-level.

**Where**: L15 conclusion (mention `obtain` as alternative), then use `obtain` in L16 or L17 so the learner practices it.

**Effort**: Medium (modify L15 conclusion + modify one later level)

**Recommend**: Yes

---

### 3. FinNavigation: Name the `Fin.lastCases` automation in the boss conclusion

**What**: Mention that Lean provides `Fin.lastCases` which automates the last-or-castSucc case split.

**Why**: MeetFin establishes an excellent pattern: teach the manual technique, then mention the automation that replaces it. L16 teaches manual case analysis on `Fin 2`, then the conclusion mentions `fin_cases` as the automated version (currently disabled). FinNavigation should follow this same pattern for the decomposition: the boss teaches the manual last-or-castSucc split, and the conclusion should mention `Fin.lastCases` (or `Fin.reverseInduction`). This contextualizes the manual work and gives the learner a name to look up later. Currently the boss conclusion never mentions any automation for this pattern.

**Where**: Boss (L10) conclusion — add a paragraph.

**Effort**: Low (sentence in conclusion)

**Recommend**: Yes

---

### 4. MeetFin: Move a concrete application earlier in the world

**What**: Add a motivational "Fin in action" note or lightweight level before L18.

**Why**: L18 "Using Fin as an Index" (level 18 of 21) is the first time the learner sees Fin used for something concrete. The preceding 17 levels are abstract manipulation: construct, extract, compare, case-split. A learner who hasn't worked with indexed data structures may lose motivation before reaching L18. The world introduction mentions vectors and polygon vertices, but no level delivers on this promise until near the end. Even a brief conclusion note in an early level (e.g., L04 or L05) connecting `Fin 5` to "the 5 positions in a poker hand" or "the 3 RGB channels" would ground the abstraction.

**Where**: L04 or L05 conclusion (low-effort note), or a new lightweight level around L08-L09.

**Effort**: Low (conclusion note) to Medium (new level)

**Recommend**: Consider

---

### 5. FinNavigation: The decomposition as a type equivalence

**What**: Note that the last-or-castSucc decomposition is really `Fin (n+1) ≃ Fin n ⊕ Unit` (or `Option (Fin n)`).

**Why**: The boss conclusion says "Fin (n+1) = image(castSucc) ⊔ {last}, disjointly" but doesn't connect this to the type-theoretic equivalence. This equivalence is *the* structural tool for recursive definitions on Fin — it's why `Fin.cons` and `Fin.snoc` work. Naming it as a type equivalence foreshadows the Fintype world (W11) where `Fintype.card_congr` uses equivalences, and the CountingTypes world (W12) where learners build equivalences explicitly. The connection is: the decomposition isn't just a proof trick — it's an isomorphism of types.

**Where**: Boss (L10) conclusion — add a paragraph connecting to `Equiv` and `Option`.

**Effort**: Low (conclusion paragraph)

**Recommend**: Yes

---

### 6. MeetFin: Note that `decide` would close many goals

**What**: Add a conclusion note explaining that `Fin n` has decidable equality and that `decide` (currently disabled) would close many of the goals in this world.

**Why**: The world disables `decide`, `fin_cases`, `norm_num`, and other automation without ever explaining what they are or why they're disabled. The learner may wonder why they're doing so much manual work. A brief note in L14 or L16 — "The `decide` tactic can close any true statement about concrete `Fin` types automatically. It's disabled in this world so you learn the underlying reasoning, but in real Lean code you'd use it freely." — would contextualize the manual work and preview the tooling.

**Where**: L14 or L16 conclusion.

**Effort**: Low (sentence in conclusion)

**Recommend**: Consider

---

### 7. FinNavigation: Succ injectivity as standalone level before boss

**What**: Give succ injectivity its own level, mirroring castSucc injectivity (L07).

**Why**: CastSucc injectivity gets a dedicated level (L07) with the full pattern spelled out. Succ injectivity uses the same pattern but is crammed into the boss's Part 2. By the time the learner reaches the boss, they're juggling the decomposition (Part 1) AND injectivity (Part 2). A standalone succ injectivity level between L07 and the boss would: (a) let the learner practice the injectivity pattern on a new function before the boss tests it, (b) make the boss less front-loaded, (c) reinforce the "same pattern, different val lemma" principle.

**Where**: New level between L07 and L08.

**Effort**: Medium (add a level, renumber L08-L10)

**Recommend**: Consider

---

### 8. MeetFin: `Fin 2 ≃ Bool` as a concrete equivalence

**What**: Make the "Fin 2 is equivalent to Bool" observation from L16's conclusion into an actual level.

**Why**: L16's conclusion says "Fun fact: Fin 2 is equivalent to Bool." This is a derivable mathematical fact, not an axiom — and it's the learner's first chance to see a non-trivial type equivalence. Building the equivalence (or just the bijection functions) would: preview `Equiv` (used heavily in W11-W12), make `Fin 2` feel concrete ("it's literally true/false"), and reinforce case analysis (since the proof requires case-splitting on Fin 2). However, `Equiv` hasn't been introduced yet and the prerequisite machinery may be too heavy.

**Where**: New level after L16 or in the conclusion of L16 with more detail.

**Effort**: High (new level requiring `Equiv` or equivalent)

**Recommend**: Consider

---

### 9. FinNavigation: `castSucc` as an order embedding

**What**: A level proving that `castSucc` preserves the ordering: `i.val ≤ j.val → i.castSucc.val ≤ j.castSucc.val`.

**Why**: L07 proves injectivity and L05 proves `castSucc < succ`. But the basic order-preservation property is never stated: that `castSucc` maps the ordering on `Fin n` to the ordering on `Fin (n+1)` faithfully. This follows trivially from `val_castSucc`, but naming it explicitly connects `Fin` to order theory and foreshadows the Orders & Lattices course. It's also a nice example of the "rewrite to values, then omega" pattern applied to an ordering statement.

**Where**: New level after L05 or L07.

**Effort**: Medium (add a level)

**Recommend**: Consider

---

### 10. MeetFin: The induction connection

**What**: In L17's conclusion (case analysis on Fin 3), note that exhaustive case analysis on `Fin n` is a form of induction on finite types.

**Why**: The conclusion of L17 says "The pattern scales: for Fin n, you get n valid cases and one impossible case." This is correct but misses the deeper point: this is *complete induction on a finite type*. In later worlds (FinsetInduction W16), the learner will do induction on finsets. Connecting manual case analysis to induction now — "What you just did is proof by cases, which is induction for finite types" — would seed the vocabulary and make the induction world feel like a natural extension rather than a new concept.

**Where**: L17 conclusion.

**Effort**: Low (sentence in conclusion)

**Recommend**: Consider

---

## Overall Impression

Both worlds are pedagogically strong. MeetFin is thorough, well-paced, and teaches every fundamental Fin operation with clear named patterns. FinNavigation builds cleanly to the decomposition theorem with good intermediate results. The writing is clear, the conclusions are substantive, and the boss levels integrate multiple moves appropriately.

The single most important improvement is **generalizing the FinNavigation decomposition to variable `n`**. The decomposition `Fin (n+1) = image(castSucc) ⊔ {last}` is the structural centerpiece of the world, and stating it only for `Fin 3` leaves the general principle implicit. MeetFin already establishes the precedent of generalizing (L11), so FinNavigation should follow suit. This is especially important because FinTuples (the next world) will need the general decomposition to explain how `Fin.cons` and `Fin.snoc` work.
